posts.title: Work-Life Balance Is a Lie. And It's Destroying You.
posts.html:
The problem isn't that you lack time. It's that your daily architecture is broken.

Last week we talked about momentum. About not putting zeros. About building mental calluses. About constructing that habit that changes your life.

But after that piece, several people asked me the same question: "All well and good, Pablo. I've got momentum — but how do I keep it without ending up in the hospital?"

The million-dollar question. The answer you want to hear: "You need perfect balance." The real answer: Balance doesn't exist.

72% of working parents report that their work constantly interferes with their family life. In practical terms, it looks like this:

  • You arrive home physically, but your head is still mentally answering emails.
  • Your kids tell you about their day and you nod on autopilot (you didn't hear a word).
  • The gym waits for "when there's spare time" (spoiler: there's never spare time).
  • You read the same page 4 times because your mental RAM is full.

I was part of that 72% for years. Today I'm not. And it's not because I achieved "balance." It's because I stopped using the scale.

THE BALANCE TRAP

Most working parents believe the solution is the famous "work-life balance" (sounds nice). They think if they divide the day into equal portions, they can give 100% in each area.

This creates a brutal cycle: You try to give 100% at the office (8-9 hours) → You arrive home wrecked → You try to force "quality time" with guilt → You sleep badly to have "time for yourself" → You repeat the cycle but more tired.

My breaking point: When Max asked me: "Dad, why are you always tired?" it hit me like a train. I realized I wasn't building an Above Average life. I was trying to survive an Average life with a nicer schedule.

Balance assumes all domains need the same energy every day. That's a lie.

THE SYSTEM: DOMAIN STACK

Forget dividing the day. The secret is stacking your 4 domains in an order that maximizes your energy, not your time. It's not time management, it's energy management.

Here's the exact system I use:

STEP 1: IDENTIFY YOUR ANCHOR

Choose one single domain that, if you execute it well, makes everything else easier or irrelevant. For me it's Health.

If I train before the sun rises:

  1. My energy for the day is already guaranteed.
  2. I've already won before the chaos begins.
  3. Everything else has a clear mind behind it.

If I win the morning with my anchor, I win the day. The rest is downhill.

STEP 2: STACK, DON'T SEPARATE

The mistake is seeing your life as separate compartments. "Now I'm an employee," "Now I'm a dad." You have to stack the domains so one feeds the next. Sometimes even in parallel if you work remotely.

My real stack:

  1. Health (5-6:30am) — charges everything else.
  2. Family (7-8am breakfast, 6-9pm evenings) — non-negotiable.
  3. Work (8am-5pm) — with full energy after the anchor.
  4. Personal projects (5-6pm) — what I build for the future.

See the difference? I don't fight for time. I flow with energy. If I tried to train at 7pm, after working, I'd arrive home dead, train badly, and have no patience for my kids. The order of factors does alter the product.

STEP 3: DISCRIMINATE WITHOUT GUILT (THE HARD PART)

This is where most people fail. To be Above Average at what matters, you have to be "bad" at what's irrelevant. You have to choose what you're going to sacrifice.

Average wants "options and freedom" — you have to become a defender of your time or this fails.

Average sacrifices things unwillingly (sleep, health, presence) and feels guilty. Above Average sacrifices consciously and sleeps peacefully.

My non-negotiable "NOs":

  • Endless scrolling: If I'm having lunch I use that time to read or advance on personal projects.
  • I don't watch TV: I cancelled cable. I was a series binge-watcher — now I watch a movie monthly with my wife in fragments. Zero news, zero channel surfing.
  • I don't go to generic "networking" events: If there's no clear ROI, I don't go.
  • I don't stay up late: 6 hours of sleep are sacred. Without sleep there's no anchor.
  • People who don't add value: Moving away from people who aren't on the same wavelength is the best thing you can do for yourself.

Am I antisocial? No. I'm focused. I know what I'm missing. But I'm crystal clear on what I'm gaining. You have to learn the art of discrimination.

THE RESULTS (REAL DATA, NOT HYPE)

Since I killed "balance" and started using the Stack:

  • Career: I work 8 hours but perform like the 12-hour days of before. Productivity +40%.
  • Family: I have breakfast and dinner with Max and Mía almost every day. Real presence, no phone at the table.
  • Health: 5-6 weekly training sessions consistently. Energy 8.5/10 stable all day.
  • Mind: ~15 books per year (before I was lucky to read 3).

I didn't sacrifice any important domain. I just eliminated the fat.

YOUR WEEKLY ACTION PLAN

Don't copy my routine. Copy the logic.

MONDAY-TUESDAY: THE ANCHOR Ask yourself: What one thing, if I do it well, improves everything else? Usually it's Health or Mind. Identify it. That's your priority #1. If that doesn't happen, the day doesn't start.

WEDNESDAY-THURSDAY: THE DESIGN Write your ideal order. Not by clock, but by energy flow.

  • What charges you? (Goes first)
  • What drains you? (Goes when you have a full tank)
  • What recovers you? (Goes at the end)

FRIDAY-SUNDAY: THE "NOT TO DO" LIST Write 3 things you're going to stop doing this week.

  • Infinite IG scroll?
  • That Tuesday meeting that could be an email?
  • Automatic Netflix after dinner? Eliminate them without guilt. You're buying freedom.

CLOSING

This week, after training, Max told me he built "the tallest building in the world" with legos. Mía spoke to me in her 2-year-old language. I gave them my eyes and ears. I was there. Then I worked 8 focused hours. Then we had dinner. Then I read.

4 domains. 1 day. No collapse. Was it perfect? No. Was it a system that works? Yes.

Mediocrity is a choice. Collapsing by chasing perfect balance is too. Choose differently.

Pablo

posts.title: Your Life Is a Frankenstein System (and It's Going to Crash)
posts.html:
THE TECHNICAL DEBT TRAP

90% of software production problems aren't new bugs.

They're old "patches" that finally collapsed.

In engineering we call it Technical Debt: the decision to take a shortcut today (easy) that you'll have to pay with interest tomorrow (hard).

"Average" lives buried in debt across their 4 vital domains:

  • Health: You eat junk today (Energy patch) → Diabetes tomorrow.
  • Family: You give your kid the iPad to shut them up (Peace patch) → Screen-addicted children.
  • Career: You postpone everything difficult (Comfort patch) → Toxic urgency on Friday, and your future self will definitely hate you.
  • Mind: Infinite scroll (Boredom patch) → Fried brain.

A hack is duct tape.

A system is architecture.

Stop patching already.

The foundations are creaking and everything is about to come down on you.

A PRODUCTION BUG (REAL STORY)

Last Tuesday. 6:30 PM.

I had a critical production problem in a SAP system.

Max (my son) came into my office screaming that he wanted to play. My brain was saturated.

The easy solution (The Hack): Give him my phone and put on YouTube Kids.

Immediate result: Silence. Peace. I could finish the email.

The real solution (The System): Close the laptop, look him in the eyes, explain that dad finishes in exactly 10 minutes and keep my word.

I chose the Hack. I chose the shortcut.

30 minutes later, when I took the phone away, hell broke loose.

Screaming, crying, chaos. I paid for those 30 minutes of "cheap peace" with 2 hours of fighting.

Sometimes exhaustion wins.

But that afternoon I remembered a technical truth:

What you don't fix at the root comes back as a critical bug at the worst moment — and costs a lot in the long run.

THE FRANKENSTEIN ANECDOTE

A few years ago I was part of building a massive healthcare management software system.

We inherited the code from another team.

It was a Frankenstein.

A rotten base, patched by 20 different hands with no documentation. Pure spaghetti code.

But the business order was: "No time to fix it. Add new features. Customize on top of what's there."

We obeyed. We built on top of the mud, and it worked (we didn't get fired) — we were even celebrated as heroes.

Result: 2 years of headaches.

Critical bugs daily. System crashes at 3 AM.

Insane maintenance costs.

Perennial chaos.

Your life today looks like that Frankenstein system.

You have rotten foundations (poor sleep, bad diet, no focus) and on top you want to add "New Features" to feel productive:

  • Running a Marathon.
  • Launching a Side Project.
  • Learning a third language.

You're customizing the disaster.

You're adding weight to a building with cracked columns.

"Average" keeps adding floors until it collapses (burnout, divorce, illness).

"Above Average" stops, goes down to the basement, and fixes the foundations.

THE SYSTEM: REFACTORING YOUR LIFE

In software, "Refactoring" doesn't add new things.

It's for cleaning, organizing, and making the system maintainable.

If you don't clean your code, you can't scale.

This is your engineering plan to get out of average:

Rule #1: Bug Audit (Brutal Honesty)

You can't fix what you ignore. Identify your "patches."

  • What recurring problem are you covering with temporary solutions?

Rule #2: Eliminate Unnecessary "Features" (Delete)

The safest code is the code that doesn't exist.

Average adds noise.

Above Average eliminates dependencies.

  • Does that meeting add real value? → Delete.
  • Simplify the stack. Fewer lines of code = fewer errors.

There's nothing more satisfying than deleting useless things from our lives.

Rule #3: Automate the "Happy Path"

Design your day so the correct flow is the only possible one.

  • Want to train? → Clothes are already ready (don't waste CPU deciding when you wake up).
  • Remove the load from the processor (your brain). Put it on the system and let it handle it.

YOUR AI AUDITOR

Identifying your own Frankenstein is hard because you've grown attached to the code keeping it alive.

Today we're going to use technology to audit yourself.

I've designed this prompt so an AI (whichever you prefer) acts as your Personal Systems Architect.

Copy, paste, and answer honestly.

The prompt guides the conversation through the 3 Rules I just mentioned.

THE "ABOVE AVERAGE REFACTORING" PROMPT:

Act as my Personal Systems Architect. Your goal is to help me identify the technical debt in my life using engineering principles.

Ask me one question at a time about each of these 3 areas:

1. BUG AUDIT: What recurring problem am I covering with a temporary solution in my health, family, career, or mind?

2. DELETE: What meeting, habit, or commitment in my week adds zero real value and should be eliminated?

3. AUTOMATE THE HAPPY PATH: What is the one action I want to do consistently that I keep deciding on each day instead of automating it?

After I answer each question, help me identify the root cause and suggest one concrete change.

Your task: Execute the prompt. Refactor. Find that critical bug. Eliminate or fix it.

Pablo

posts.title: Your Willpower Has a Bug (AI Is the Patch)
posts.html:
Why I stopped trusting my "desire" after 19 marathons.

Motivation is the biggest "smoke" in the personal development industry.

They sold us the idea that to do great things, we have to feel inspired.

That's a lie.

And it's a dangerous lie.

"Average" (the average professional) operates under this scheme:

  1. Wakes up needing motivation.
  2. Looks for it on YouTube/Instagram.
  3. Gets a dopamine hit.
  4. Feels productive for 2 hours.
  5. Crashes. Repeat.

Do you know who doesn't have bad days?

The Algorithm.

Do you know who doesn't need to watch a YouTube video to get to work?

Artificial Intelligence.

I'm not telling you to become a robot.

I'm telling you to use the robot to protect yourself from your own biology.

The Invisible Enemy: Decision Fatigue

There's a physiological reason why at 10 AM you're a lion and at 3 PM you're a zombie scrolling Instagram.

It's called Decision Fatigue.

Your prefrontal cortex has a limited "battery."

Every decision you make consumes charge.

  • What do I wear? (-5%)
  • What do I eat for breakfast? (-5%)
  • Do I answer this email or that one? (-10%)

By the time it's time to do deep work, your battery is in the red.

The Lesson of 42 Kilometers

I've been running marathons since 2011. 19 of them.

People ask me: "Pablo, how do you feel like running 30km on a Sunday at 5am in winter?"

The answer always disappoints: I almost never feel like it.

If it depended on my desire, I'd stay in bed buried under the covers.

But I don't depend on my desire.

I depend on my System.

  • The sneakers are next to the bed.
  • The clothes are prepared.
  • The Garmin already has the workout loaded.

I don't decide. The system decided last night.

I'm just the worker executing the architect's order.

And I know that system conditions me toward a better version of myself every time I execute it.

That's your real ROI.

AI: Your Digital Architect

This is where technology comes in. Many people use AI to generate text. That's basic.

"Above Average" uses AI to generate structure.

I use AI to eliminate the friction of decision-making.

I delegate planning, prioritization, and ordering to it.

It's the logical machine that doesn't get tired, doesn't stress, and doesn't get scared.

It builds the path. I walk it.

The result: When I sit down to work, I don't spend a single watt of energy thinking "what do I do?"

I just execute.

The Real Why (Legacy)

And this, at the end of the day, isn't about productivity. It's about Reliability.

When you close the laptop, you're a father, a spouse, a person of faith.

  • Your kids don't need a "motivated" father. They need a present father.
  • Your faith doesn't ask for "emotion." It asks for commitment.

Motivation comes and goes.

Systems remain. If you want to leave a legacy, stop waiting to "feel good."

You're human to love.

You have to be machine to deliver.

Pablo | Above Average

posts.title: I Fired My Coach. Hired an AI. (The 42km Experiment)
posts.html:
80% of professional parents who try to run a marathon break down or quit before the starting line.

It's not because they lack discipline. It's because they're using a static plan for a dynamic life.

I'm 40+, with 19 marathons behind me and a Tech Lead job. And I'm going to tell you an uncomfortable truth: Paying a "human" coach to send you a static PDF over WhatsApp is throwing money away.

Don't get me wrong — there are great coaches out there. But they're expensive and most don't specialize in profiles like ours (parents with an exploding schedule).

Most treat you like an elite athlete with free time, or send you the same generic plan they send everyone ("Average").

For my last two marathons, and especially for Buenos Aires this year, I did something many considered crazy: I fired the humans and put my preparation in the hands of Artificial Intelligence.

The result?

  • Crossed the finish line in 3h:14m (my second-best time ever, right on the edge of qualifying for Boston).
  • Didn't get divorced.
  • Didn't get fired.
  • Arrived in one piece.

The Hell of the Rigid Plan

A few months ago I was in "amateur runner hell." I had a plan stuck to the fridge that said: "Tuesday: 12km with Fartlek."

But my real Tuesday was:

  1. Max sick → took him to the doctor.
  2. Emergency production meeting.
  3. Mía refused to sleep until 11pm.

I looked at the plan on the fridge. The plan looked at me. I felt that toxic guilt of someone who "doesn't follow through." I went running anyway, slept 5 hours, and the next day I was a zombie in the standup.

That's when I said: "Enough."

It can't be that at work I use Agile methodologies that adapt to change, but for my health I use a "Waterfall" paper system that ignores my reality.

I decided to hack the system.

Rigidity Is Fragility

They sold us the lie that discipline means "doing what the paper says no matter what." And don't you dare question it.

That's mathematically impossible if you're a parent with responsibilities.

Life's entropy always beats your spreadsheet.

The truth nobody tells you: Rigidity isn't strength.

Rigidity is fragility. If your plan breaks because you had an extra meeting, your plan is garbage.

Average forces reality to fit the plan (and breaks down).

Above Average creates a Dynamic System that adapts to reality in real time.

You don't need more willpower. You need more adaptability.

Adaptive Training Protocol with AI

Here's how I built my own system. It's not magic. It's data engineering applied to the body. I used Gemini (you can use Claude or ChatGPT) and created a specific "Gem" with these 3 steps:

1. Context Ingestion (The Master Prompt): Don't ask "give me a marathon plan." That's amateur. You need to give it your hard constraints. My initial prompt included:

"I'm X years old. I weigh X kg. My VO2 Max is X. I can train 4 days, not 6. My heavy work days are Thursdays. My best recent half marathon time was X. Goal: Buenos Aires Marathon in 16 weeks."

That's the simple version — the more detailed you make it, the better. Take that output to an Excel or Notion. That's your map, but the map isn't the territory.

2. The Daily Feedback Loop: Every morning, I feed the AI my recovery metrics (HRV and Sleep from Garmin) and my schedule.

Prompt: "I slept badly today (Score 40) and have 3 hours of back-to-back meetings. Adjust today's training so I don't burn out."

3. The Live Recalculation ("Recalculating..."): If I couldn't train on Tuesday, the AI doesn't make me feel guilty. It recalculates the weekly volume and redistributes it.

The secret: I manage 3 horizons (Optimistic, Realistic, Pessimistic). If the week gets complicated, the AI switches to the "Pessimistic" plan to save minimum volume without breaking me. The important thing is to arrive at the start line and complete the marathon in the worst case.

Result: Zero Burnout

I ran the 42km of Buenos Aires achieving my best time in 10 years. But the real number isn't on the clock.

The real number is: ZERO BURNOUT.

  • I always maintained my energy to play with Max and Mía.
  • I didn't drop my performance on work and personal projects.
  • My injuries stayed controlled because AI stopped me when I (out of ego) wanted to accelerate.

Cost: $20 USD subscription. Gain: Peace of mind and health.

The free versions of any AI will never give you the "care" that a paid one does.

The cost of 4 coffees per month on something that can literally change your life in many ways and make you Above Average — it's worth it.

Maybe all this works for me because I'm a "Lone Wolf." My schedule doesn't allow for going to the trendy Running Club to socialize. I need pure efficiency.

Your Turn

Coaches aren't going to disappear. If you need someone to yell "come on!" and send you motivational stickers over WhatsApp, pay for one.

But if you know what you're doing (gym, running, triathlon) and want to optimize your life, delegate planning to AI.

Your task for today: Take your current plan and feed it to AI with this prompt:

"Act as my high-performance coach. I'm 40+, have kids and little time. Optimize this plan to be sustainable, not ideal. Prioritize longevity over maximum performance."

Stop training for the ideal scenario that never happens.

Train for the life you have — and be Above Average.

See you on the road.

Pablo Marichal Above Average

posts.title: AI Is the New Philosopher's Stone (and That's a Problem)
posts.html:
I just finished reading "Transcend" by Faisal Hoque, and honestly it blew my mind with a historical analogy that perfectly explains where we stand today.

Hoque says AI is the modern version of the Philosopher's Stone.

Remember alchemy? It was that mythical substance people desperately sought because it could turn lead into gold and grant eternal life.

Basically, it was the promise of getting everything you want without effort.

But ancient texts had one key warning:

If you're not mentally and spiritually prepared, that power corrupts or destroys you.

It's not that the stone is "bad." It's that if you use it only to avoid work, you become weak.

The BA Success and the Thin Line

I'm telling you this because I lived it firsthand.

As you know, this year I outsourced my planning for the Buenos Aires Marathon to AI.

The result? A success.

It removed mental load, optimized timing, and worked perfectly as a tool.

For executing a plan, AI is a beast.

But that's the trap. When you see it works so well for optimizing, you start getting the itch to use it to avoid effort in everything else. And that's where you go from using the tool to the tool using you.

The real danger isn't that AI is Terminator. The danger is that it becomes your Super Nanny and infantilizes you.

The Code vs. the "Magic Pill"

The other afternoon I could hear Max and Mía playing on the other side of the house. I was in front of the computer reviewing a complex SAP problem.

I had two paths:

  1. Ask AI: "Analyze this SAP class and tell me what it does and how to fix it." Wait 30 seconds.
  2. Read the ABAP code myself, debug it step by step, understand the architecture. Take 45 minutes.

Option 1 is the "Philosopher's Stone." Gold without effort. But Above Average chose option 2.

Why? Because if I let the machine always solve the hard problems of my work, I become a Junior with steroids.

The day the server crashes, the AI hallucinates nonsense, or the client asks for something not in the database — I'll be useless.

The value of my career isn't the final code; it's the mental callus that forms in my brain when I solve the problem myself.

The System: Detach & Devote

In the book, Hoque proposes a framework ("Detach and Devote") to not lose humanity in this dance.

I brought it down to earth for my operating system:

DETACH (Let go of toxic comfort) We have to "detach" from the idea that everything has to be easy or convenient thanks to AI magic.

  • Never delegate your judgment: Use AI to find data, not to make the final decision for you.
  • Never delegate the sweat: AI builds the plan (like in BA), but you have to run the 42km yourself. If you look for the shortcut in effort, you lose.

DEVOTE (Give yourself to the irreplaceable) The time you save with AI isn't to "kill time." It's to "devote yourself" to elevating your potential.

  • Real connection: Look people in the eyes, listen without your phone in hand, handle a difficult conversation. Don't lean on AI to avoid being genuinely human.
  • Creativity from scratch: Grab a blank sheet and create something without prompts, just to keep the muscle from atrophying. The other day I wrote by hand and my hand hurt — I realized we're atrophying even there.
  • Purpose: AI doesn't have "whys" — only patterns. You decide where the ship is headed. Think of it as a compass, but if you don't know how to use one, you'll end up anywhere.

The Action: The Comfort Fast

This week, I challenge you to recover your edge.

Choose one task you normally "outsource" to technology (to AI, to Google, to GPS) and do it yourself, the old way.

  • Write that important report from scratch — at least one email.
  • Navigate the city without GPS for a day. Try to remember an address without Waze.
  • Calculate your running pace in your head, run by feel.

Feel the friction. Feel it costing you something. That's where you're really alive.

What isn't used atrophies.

AI is the magic stone and it solves our life in many ways.

Use it to build, not to stop working.

It's not an easy decision in this world of immediacy.

But that's where the judgment that differentiates us from the rest lives.

That's how we become Above Average.

Pablo / Above Average

posts.title: Post-Mortem 2025: Why Your Goals Will Fail
posts.html:
It's December 21st.

10 days left until the calendar changes version.

I can already see it coming. Soon the avalanche of fateful posts on LinkedIn and Instagram will begin: "New Year, New Me", "This is my year", "Blank page".

Please, don't be one more of the crowd.

Don't fall into the trap of the 99% who write a wish list on a napkin or in their phone notes:

"I'm going to lose 10 pounds." "I'm going to read 12 books." "I'm going to be more patient with the kids."

As a Tech Lead, I have to tell it to you raw: That's not a plan — it's a hallucination. It's a simple wish that dies before it compiles.

The data doesn't lie: According to Strava, "Quitter's Day" (the day most people abandon their resolutions) is the second Friday of January. That "motivation" lasts less than a poorly planned sprint.

If in your work a production server crashes, we don't light a candle wishing "next year it won't crash." We don't trust luck.

We do a Post-Mortem.

We read the logs. We find the Root Cause. We write the patch. We deploy the fix.

If you want 2026 to be different, stop dreaming and start debugging your 2025.

The "Goals" Bug (Scott Adams)

In his book How to Fail at Almost Everything and Still Win Big, Scott Adams destroys the idea of goals with an engineer's logic:

"Losers have goals. Winners have systems."

The structural problem with a goal (e.g., "Run a marathon") is that you live in a state of constant failure until the moment you achieve it. And if you get injured the day before, all the effort feels like a system crash. It's fragile.

A System (e.g., "Run 30 minutes every day at 6 AM") changes the reward. Every time you go out, the code ran well. You won. The system gives you daily dopamine. The marathon stops being the objective and becomes an inevitable side effect.

Antifragility: Using the Chaos (Nassim Taleb)

This is where most professional parents fail. We look for "stability," but life with kids and corporate work is pure chaos.

Nassim Taleb explains in Antifragile that your 2025 review isn't to punish yourself. It's to extract data.

Every mistake of this year, every day you didn't follow through, every argument you lost — is Data. If you ignore that data, 2026 will have the same bugs as 2025.

Your Sunday Task: The RCA (Root Cause Analysis)

So you don't stay in theory, I designed a tool. It's the same one I use.

It's not a colorful wish list. It's a cold spreadsheet based on the "5 Whys" technique we use in the industry to find the origin of a failure.

How the tool works:

  1. Look at each of your 4 vital domains (Health, Family, Career, Mind).
  2. For each failed goal, ask "Why?" five times until you find the real root cause.
  3. Write the patch — a concrete system, not a new wish.

Download it here*: The Annual Debugging (v1.0) Above Average

*Google will ask you to create your own private copy. Your data is not shared with anyone.

Do the dirty work today.

Look at your year's logs in the face. Your future self will thank me.

It's the only way for the 2026 version of yourself to be an upgrade — not just a restart.

Pablo Marichal | Tech Lead | Above Average

posts.title: Your Life Is a Legacy System (Migration Manual 2026)
posts.html:
Most 40+ technology professionals operate like a SAP R/3 instance from 1999.

Robust systems, yes. They've processed millions of transactions (crises, projects, kids, debts). But over the years, they filled up with "Z Code": custom patches, temporary solutions that became permanent, and spaghetti logic that nobody dares touch for fear everything will crash.

The system works, but it's slow. It's rigid. And worst of all: it's incompatible with the speed of AI. While you process a nightly batch, the market already runs in real time.

If you try to run a Marathon (High Performance) or learn AI (Innovation) on that obsolete infrastructure, the result is inevitable: PRODUCTION DUMP. (Burnout, injury, or family crisis.)

In the SAP world, the answer to this is the "Clean Core" strategy. Keep the standard nucleus clean and updated, and move innovation to an external layer (BTP).

In plain terms: A standard system that avoids toxic customizations at the core. At the human level, the same applies. If you want functional longevity and technical relevance, you have to stop "tuning" your life and start cleaning the core from the inside.

This is the Clean Core Migration Protocol for the Full Stack Human.

PHASE 1: CODE AUDIT (Code Inspector)

Before optimizing, you have to clean. Do a trace of your week. Where are you losing CPU cycles? If you're brutally honest, you'll probably find these processes running:

  • ZBADSLEEP: Sleeping 5 hours to "be productive"? That's technical debt with compound interest. The invoice always arrives.
  • ZJUNKFOOD: Eating processed food because you "don't have time"? You're corrupting the database. You're a time bomb — there's no health insurance that covers a system without maintenance.
  • Z_POLITICS: 2-hour meetings without an agenda? Processes in an infinite loop that consume mental RAM. Stop wasting your time — poorly invested time has no rollback.

Action: Identify 3 "Z programs" you're running in the background and kill them (kill process).

PHASE 2: THE KERNEL (Basis Administration)

In a Clean Core, the Kernel is untouchable. Non-negotiable. It's what keeps the system up. Your biological Kernel has three hardcoded parameters:

  1. Sleep: 7-8 hours. The minimum Support Package for the brain.
  2. Movement: 30+ minutes daily. Without it, the processor throttles.
  3. Nutrition: Real food. Garbage in, garbage out.

If you modify the standard of these three, you lose manufacturer support (your biology). You have to return to the standard regardless. And watch out: staying current requires constantly applying Support Packages.

AI is going to automate everything that's repetitive. Your only competitive advantage is your creative cognitive capacity. And you can't have mental clarity if your biological Kernel is running on emergency. Without health, you're easily automatable. You become Deprecated.

PHASE 3: EXTENSIBILITY (Side-by-Side Innovation)

This is where most people get it wrong. They try to mix work, sport, and family into one shapeless mass. The Clean Core model uses decoupling.

  • Your Family (Standard Code): It's the heart of the ERP. Stability, values, presence. Don't try to "optimize" your kids. Give them dedicated bandwidth.
  • Sport (Extension 1): Whether it's Marathon, Triathlon, or gym. It's a high-performance application running on the Kernel. If the Kernel is strong, you can handle 42km. If it's weak, the app crashes the system. Stop being sedentary — sedentarism is a critical bug.
  • AI/Tech (Extension 2): Your ability to learn Copilot or S/4 HANA depends on your mental clarity. A clean core processes new information faster.

THE MIGRATION (Go-Live 2026)

You can't do a Big Bang. Do it in phases:

  • Q1 (Jan-Mar): Stabilize the Basis. Focus only on sleeping and eating clean. No personal records yet.
  • Q2 (Apr-Jun): Deploy Extensions. Start strong training block. Get deep into AI now — tomorrow it's already too late.
  • Q3 onward: Continuous Innovation. With a stable system, go for High Performance. Sequence: Family > Body > Learning.

Stop living in Legacy mode. Planned obsolescence is for machines, not for us.

AI won't replace humans. Humans with a Clean Core, using AI, will replace humans who keep operating like a zombie R/3. Become more human — it's the only thing that can't be programmed.

Clean your Core. Maintain the standard. Innovate at the edges. You were made to perform above average.

Your migration starts now. First project task: Close the screens and sleep 8 hours tonight. Execute and stop making excuses.

Pablo "Above Average" Marichal.

posts.title: Above Average OS v1.0: Why brute force is failing (DeepSeek vs. You)
posts.html:
Why Brute Force is failing and how to install the new OS.

The Signal: Architecture > Brute Force

Welcome to the new standard.

If you have been reading me for the past 11 weeks, you noticed a change. We switched languages. This is not a random choice. The battle against human obsolescence is global.

The standard of the Tech Industry is English. To compete at the highest level, we must align with the standard.

Today, we launch Above Average OS v1.0.

And to understand why this matters, we need to look at what happened in the AI world just 96 hours ago.

1. The End of the "Muscle Head" Era

On January 1, 2026, DeepSeek (the Chinese AI lab) released a paper that quietly humiliated the rest of the industry.

For years, the strategy in AI was "Brute Force":

  • Want a smarter model? Buy more GPUs.
  • Want better reasoning? Burn more electricity.
  • Want to beat OpenAI? Spend more billions.

This was the "Muscle Head Era." Pure, raw power (and a lot of money wasted).

But DeepSeek had a problem: they faced heavy export controls. They couldn't just "buy more power" (Nvidia H100s were restricted). So they were forced to think differently.

They published the "Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections" (mHC) framework.

Without getting bogged down in the math, here is the lesson: Instead of adding more hardware, they optimized the internal architecture of the network. They made the "brain" efficient, so it didn't need as much "muscle."

The Result: They achieved state-of-the-art performance at a fraction of the cost.

The Lesson: Clever Architecture beats brute force.

2. Are you running on Brute Force?

This is exactly where most Senior Professionals (+40) are failing today. We are trying to survive modern life using the "Muscle Head" strategy:

  • Work is overwhelming? --> we brute force it (Work late, skip lunch, sleep 5 hours).
  • Health is declining? --> we brute force it (Extreme diets, "David Goggins" mode for 2 weeks, then crash).
  • Family is demanding? --> we brute force it (Multitasking, being physically present but mentally absent).

We are trying to fix a Software Problem (bad habits, lack of focus, poor prioritization) with Hardware (more hours, more coffee, more stress).

It is unsustainable. Eventually, the hardware breaks, you already know that.

In engineering, we call it "Catastrophic Failure."

In life, we call it Burnout, Divorce, or a Heart Attack.

3. The Threat: The Vegetalization of Humanity

While we exhaust ourselves with brute force, the machines are taking over our cognitive load.

I have spent the last year devouring every single book by philosopher Éric Sadin. From “L'Intelligence artificielle ou l'enjeu du siècle” to his latest essays. Reading him completely rewired my perspective on what we do for a living.

As tech professionals, we are often blinded by the "utility" of the tool. We obsess over efficiency. But Sadin exposes the hidden cost: The Administration of Existence.

He argues that when we let algorithms guide every decision, from what route to drive (Waze) to what code to write (Copilot) to what to watch (Netflix), we are not "optimized." We are neutralized.

We are witnessing the "Vegetalization of the human subject."

A state where we are no longer "Agents" acting on the world, but merely passive nodes receiving instructions from a system.

The Danger of the "AI Slooper" There is a new term floating around: the "AI Slooper." It refers to the lazy user who blindly loops prompts, accepts the first output, and lets the LLM do the thinking.

  • The Slooper asks AI to write the email and sends it without reading.
  • The Slooper asks AI to solve the bug and pastes the code without understanding the logic.

This is exactly what Sadin warns about. When you bypass the friction of thinking, you atrophy the muscle of judgment.

Our mandate as tech people: we cannot be luddites. We must use AI. I use it every day to leverage my output. But there is a thin line between leverage and dependency.

  • Leverage is using AI to run faster.
  • Dependency is forgetting how to walk.

If you are a Tech Lead today, your job is to master the tool without becoming its pet. You must "out-architect" DeepSeek. You must reclaim your agency.

4. The Solution: The "Full-Stack Human" OS

I am transitioning from a standard Tech Professional to a Full-Stack Human. I stopped trying to "hustle." I started engineering my life like a mission-critical system.E

We need to be future-proof.

The Above Average OS is the framework I use to ensure I don't become "Legacy Code." It has 3 Layers: 1. 2. 3.

Stop forcing. Start architecting.

Pablo Marichal Tech Lead | 19x Marathoner

posts.title: I sold my bike to buy a career (a Venezuela story)
posts.html:
TL;DR for the Busy:

  • The Context: Venezuela’s collapse forced me to pivot or perish.
  • The Sacrifice: I sold my bike to pay for an SAP certification.
  • The Lesson: Sometimes you have to kill your ego (Senior -> Rookie) and burn the ships to survive a silent war.

Venezuela is on everyone’s lips right now. It is impossible to ignore the headlines. And for me, impossible not to look back.

I was born in Uruguay, but I became an immigrant at age 10. Venezuela was the country that raised me. It received us with open arms, as it did with everyone in its golden age, before the systematic destruction began.

To understand the magnitude of the tragedy, you only need to look at one chart: Poland vs. Venezuela. In the early 90s, they had similar GDP trajectories. Today, they are galaxies apart. One is a thriving European economy; the other has 90% critical poverty and a minimum wage of $3.

Just take a look at this graph and draw your own conclusions:

2 different realities today...

But I have to be fair: I owe my career to Venezuela. I studied Computer Engineering for free. That education allowed me to become a Microsoft Consultant while I was still in university and be part of a vibrant tech community.

Those credentials got me into a massive state-owned heavy industry company. Back then, landing that job was like winning the lottery. You were set for life. But then, the rules changed. The government "demonized" proprietary software.

Suddenly, we were forced to migrate everything to Open Source. I had to adapt fast. I learned Linux, PHP, Ruby on Rails, and open standards out of necessity, not choice. That chaos made me a Full Stack Developer by survival.

The Trap and The Pivot

By 2015, the deterioration reached a breaking point. It wasn't that the system suddenly started rewarding mediocrity. That had been a slow, agonizing rot for years. But by then, it became unbearable.

You couldn't grow in the big state-owned companies unless you played the political game. Technical merit was irrelevant; allegiance to the regime was the only KPI that mattered. We were being directed by the worst technical managers alive, political appointees with zero expertise who were driving the industry into the ground.

I found myself in a professional death trap. But I saw a way out: SAP.

There was an SAP team in the company that operated differently. They were a "High-Performance" island. Autonomous, professional, and somehow immune to the toxic politics surrounding us. I wanted in. But to join that "Dream Team," I had to start from zero. I was already a Senior Full Stack Dev. I had status. I had to kill my ego, forget my Seniority, and become a Rookie again.

Oh, and they had one rule: You had to be certified. And naturally, there was zero budget for training.

I made a deal with them: I would learn on my own for 5 months, then go to Caracas for a month to attend an on-site academy and take the certification exam.

The Asset

Then came the reality check: I had to pay for the month in Caracas (hotel, food, academy). I lived 700 kms from there. I needed cash, and the economy was collapsing. I wasn't a marathon winner yet (it’s a joke, I didn't do an internship in Kenya), so I didn't have sponsors. But I had one asset: My bike.

I had bought it a year earlier to get into triathlons (which was a total failure, by the way). So, it didn’t hurt too much to let it go. I sold the bike. With that money, I financed my own "Scholarship."

The ROI

I went to Caracas focused 100% on SAP, and passed. It sounds easy now. Today, learning SAP is democratic: you have free Learning Journeys, trial environments, and AI tutors. Back then, access was elitist and scarce.

That pivot saved me. It allowed me to work as a Basis/ABAP consultant (and sometimes as TPM) until I could finally leave the country in 2018.

The Silent War

I recently told this story to a group of European VPs and Managers at my current company. I saw a mix of amusement and admiration in their eyes (and received great feedback later). One of them told me it reminded him of the stories his grandfather told about the war in Europe 80 years ago. Stories of trading heirlooms for food or passage.

And that struck me. Leaving wasn't a choice; it was a forced migration. I delayed my departure to help my parents escape first. When my turn came, I had to sell everything for pennies. My car. My house. But the hardest part was giving away my books. I had a library of technical and literature books that I loved. Nobody wanted them. Seeing my intellectual capital (and one of my hobbies) being given away because they were "too heavy" to carry was the moment I realized:

Material things are temporary. Only what you carry in your mind is yours.

Don't take it for granted...

I returned to Uruguay and started from zero, again. Knowing no one in the industry. As a rookie, earning just a bit more than minimum wage.

But there is a concept from Naval Ravikant that always resonated with me: If you strip a resourceful person of all their money and drop them in a random English-speaking country, within 5 years they will be wealthy again.

Why? Because wealth isn't the money in the bank.

Wealth is the skill to generate it.

That is exactly what I proved (even though I landed in Uruguay, English remained my professional leverage).

I lost my material assets (house, car, books), but I kept the "Source Code." In less than five years, I rebuilt my life from scratch. I don't know if Uruguay is my final stop. In a global world, you never know. But I know that wherever I go, my Operating System comes with me.

My old environment didn't corrupt me. I came from a toxic system, but I kept my agency.

It is a matter of attitude and focus. Today, I work for a global company where I have been recognized as a "High Performer" for three consecutive years. The collapse didn't define me; my response to it did.

A Note to the Observer

If this story resonated with those European managers as a "war story," it is because it is one. Venezuela has been in a silent war for a long time. But it was a war where only one side had the guns, and the other side, the people, has been oppressed since Day 1.

So, if you haven't lived through it, be careful with your opinions. It is easy to judge from the comfort of a stable democracy.

To my Venezuelan brothers and sisters: I am with you. I hope one day the country heals enough for me to take my children there, so they can see where their father was forged.

Why am I telling you this?

Because whether it's a country collapsing, massive layoffs, or AI disrupting your industry in 2026, the rule remains the same: The environment is volatile.

Your Operating System, must be antifragile.

You cannot control the macro (politics, AI, the economy). You can only control the micro (your agency, your skills, your response).

I built the Above Average OS to document the systems I use to navigate chaos without losing my sanity, or my family.

Don't rely on luck. Build your own raft. Be ready for anything. Be future-proof.

posts.title: The MWD Trap (and Why Ownership Is the Only Way Out)
posts.html:
Last week I told the story here of how I sold my bicycle to escape Venezuela's entropy and finance my entry into the SAP world. The reaction was positive. Many connected with the emotional part of the sacrifice: "burning the boats" to survive — or simply out of empathy for everything happening in Venezuela.

But today, coffee in hand and with more space to think, I want to talk about what happened after selling the bike. Because that sale didn't just pay for a certification and an entry into SAP. It paid for my identity change at a professional level.

That day I stopped operating as an employee seeking "security" and started operating as an operational partner seeking "systems."

I learned a lesson that goes against everything traditional business school taught you:

To truly grow, you have to work every day to make yourself "dispensable" in operations.

This is where most people fail, falling into two deadly traps:

1. The MWD Trap (Minimum Work Done)

In software we love the MVP (Minimum Viable Product). But in offices, the MWD (Minimum Work Done) is everywhere.

If you've never heard of MWD, that's fine — I invented it (search Google and if you find that definition with that acronym, I'll buy the coffee).

It's the law of least effort. The professional who does just enough to not get fired.

  • "I'm not documenting this because I can't be bothered."
  • "I'm not improving the process because nobody asked me to."
  • The one who comes into meetings, says hi, and nothing else.
  • The one you know will complicate anything you ask and is always "busy."

The MWD practitioner becomes a drag. They rust. And when a restructuring (or an AI) comes, they're the first to fall because they provide no differential value.

They're the supporting character who manages to lower the morale of the entire team, because everyone knows they do little to nothing but keep getting paid. And no — this isn't something that only lives in the public sector. You see these individuals in the corporate world too.

2. The "Irreplaceable" Trap (The Black Box)

On the other side is the person who works hard, but wrong. They believe their job security depends on being the only one with the key.

  • "I'm not going to teach this, so they always have to call me."
  • "If I automate my job, what do I do after?"
  • These are the artists of "throwing a wrench in the works."
  • Every time you ask them something, you come out with more questions than answers, because they want to seem like they know more than they do but never provide optimal solutions to the problem.

Let me be brutally honest:

If you're irreplaceable in daily operations, you're not a strategic asset. You're a risk liability.

In finance, a liability is a debt. In systems, it's a threat. If the entire operation depends on you being in your chair, you're a Single Point of Failure (in plain terms, a bottleneck). It's like having a critical server with no backup.

And if you're a bottleneck:

  1. You can't go on vacation without everything breaking.
  2. You become the scapegoat for every incident.
  3. You make yourself impossible to promote — because who's going to move you if everything depends on you?

Both the slacker (MWD) and the hoarder (Black Box) end up in the same place: stuck.

The Way Out: Extreme Ownership (Taking Charge)

At Above Average, we operate under a different philosophy. I call it "Always ready to quit."

Watch out: This doesn't mean threatening to leave. It means building systems so robust, documented, and autonomous that if tomorrow you win the lottery, operations continue running perfectly without you.

Some cynic will read this and think: "Documenting everything and giving away my knowledge... that's being the company's doormat, that's being a pushover."

Wrong. That's being a true professional. And being professional isn't about certifications or titles — it's about having this type of attitude.

At the end of the day, you're paid to solve problems, not to kidnap them. If your idea of "rebellion" is hiding information and doing the minimum, I invite you to found your own company and hire people with that mindset. You'll see how fast you go under.

We don't do this out of charity or to be liked by the boss. We do it for market competitiveness. Operating with excellence is the only way to keep your value high. It's strategic selfishness.

What does this look like in practice?

  • Obsessive documentation: If a junior asks me something twice, I failed. It means there's no clear manual or video. Documentation isn't bureaucracy — it's legacy.
  • Democratization of knowledge: The old school said "information is power." The new school says "shared information is speed." I train my team by example to do what I do.
  • Automation: If it's repetitive, a script or AI should do it, not me.

The Value Paradox

Here's the magic: When you make yourself "dispensable" in operational tasks (because you created a system that handles them), you automatically become INDISPENSABLE in strategy.

The company doesn't pay you to press buttons. It pays you to design the machine that presses the buttons. You go from being an operator to being an Architect. And that's where the true value of your professional work lies.

The 2026 Factor: AI Has No Mercy

Why do I insist on this today? Because Artificial Intelligence is coming for the middle. AI is excellent at repetitive tasks (where the MWD lives) and processing retained data (where the Irreplaceable lives).

But AI can't replace Ownership. It can't replace the human who takes charge (the best translation of the concept), who has ethical judgment, who leads teams, and who has the vision to build resilient systems.

The Source Code

When I sold my bike and left Venezuela, I lost my material things and was left with a couple of suitcases. But I took with me the "Source Code": my capacity to learn and to solve problems.

Tomorrow, when you sit down in front of the screen, ask yourself these 2 questions:

  1. Am I doing this to avoid being noticed (MWD), or am I doing it to build something that lasts?
  2. Am I hoarding knowledge to feel irreplaceable, or am I sharing it to build systems that set me free?

Don't seek to be needed through inefficiency. Seek to be chosen through excellence.

The 99% of the world only consumes. Be part of the 1% that generates value. That's how you build a legacy.

Keep being better than average.

**Pablo / Above Average

posts.title: The Competitive Advantage of Going Back to Being a Rookie (and My Plan Z)
posts.html:
In 2017, my life fit in two suitcases.

I was in the middle of selling everything, closing accounts, and saying goodbye to my family to leave Venezuela and restart my professional life from scratch. Amid that emotional and logistical chaos, I wrote an article on Medium with a reflection that today, 9 years later, hits me in the face like a brick:

"When we get old, we're afraid of learning new things. And that kills us."

In that 2017 text, I mentioned as a "distant curiosity" the news that two Facebook bots had invented their own language to communicate and engineers had to shut them down. Back then it seemed like science fiction, a fun anecdote for a barbecue.

Today I reread that and it doesn't make me laugh. It gives me urgency.

Last week the tech industry shook again. We see tools like Claude Code (with Opus 4.5) being more than simple "chatbots" that write you a poem — doing the work of the world's best programmers. They're agents. They write code, refactor, deploy, and fix bugs in the terminal while we go get coffee.

Yesterday for example I installed clawd.bot on a free AWS layer. Just a matter of running a script in the terminal to install, selecting the model, and starting to throw prompts with context. Out of nowhere, you have an agent working for you 24x7. Total madness within anyone's reach.

Well, within "anyone's" reach who knows how to use it. Because the risk isn't AI — the risk is stagnating while it evolves.

At the same time, the "Vegetalization of Humanity" that philosopher Éric Sadin talks about (I bought all his books) is no longer a French academic theory; it's a daily occupational risk. We're becoming passive spectators of an intelligence that doesn't rest, doesn't take vacation days, and doesn't make errors from tiredness.

If you think this is an unfair competition, you're right: it is. But nobody said life was fair. It is what it is and you have to row.

99% of people today only consume AI. The challenge is to be part of the 1% that masters it.

Another Day Feeling Like an Impostor (and Liking It)

Over the weekend I watched Google DeepMind's documentary about AlphaFold. For those who don't know, it's the AI that solved the "protein folding problem" — a biological mystery that had science stuck for 50 years. Basically, they deciphered the building blocks of life.

Watching Demis Hassabis (the brain behind DeepMind) and his team accomplish in months what would have taken humanity centuries generated two things in me.

First, fascination. What a privilege to live in the same era as these people. Second, being brutally honest: I felt like an ignorant next to this person.

I felt stupid. We think we're "Senior" because we know how to configure an ERP or lead a migration, and suddenly I realized that my technical knowledge — the kind it took years to accumulate — was becoming a commodity.

It's human nature to compare. But here there were two paths: get depressed, become defensive and deny reality (we know how that story ends), or embrace that feeling.

The title of "Senior" is a trap. The "Senior" believes they've arrived, that they're a star for life. The "Senior" defends what they know. The "Senior" is afraid to ask questions to avoid looking stupid.

The "Senior" is on borrowed time if they don't have hunger to learn every day.

Why Go Back to School?

I could stay comfortable in my Plan A, pretending my experience shields my future. But the documentary awakened something I'd already sensed 9 years ago. If DeepMind's geniuses didn't stop until they solved proteins, I can't stop until I understand this new playing field. Or at least not remain in ostracism without putting up a fight.

So I made a counterintuitive decision: Go back to the "Rookie Mindset."

Instead of doing another postgraduate program or signing up for a theoretical MBA to hang a diploma on the wall and feed my ego, I enrolled in Platzi.

Yes, I went back to the basics. Back to being a student alongside 20-year-olds. Why? Because I don't have time for five-year-old academic theory. I need to know what's happening today in the trenches. Although I keep a close eye on the SAP+AI world, being in a study program forces you to discipline.

I need to understand how these agents work (which aren't just Copilot or SAP Joule), how they integrate, how they operate. I need it to cost me something. I need to feel that frustration of not understanding anything at first.

The good news is that being a marathon runner, I have tolerance for pain. I got used to not throwing in the towel. I know I'll manage even when it's hard. And when I say "hard," I mean sacrificing free time to keep training the neurons and stay ready in case I need to burn the boats again.

That discomfort of "feeling stupid" while learning something new is, paradoxically, the only thing that makes me feel intelligent today.

Plan Z (Reid Hoffman Was Right)

In that "Nostradamus" article I also wrote in 2017, I cited the book "The Start-up of You" by Reid Hoffman (LinkedIn's founder). He says that as professionals, we're a company in "permanent beta" and need three plans:

  • Plan A: What you do today. Your comfort zone.
  • Plan B: Your lateral pivot. Where you move if A fails?
  • Plan Z: Your life raft. The certainty that if everything burns down tomorrow, you have the raw skills to survive.

In 2017, my Plan Z was emigrating from a collapsing country. In 2026, my Plan Z is intellectual humility.

My life insurance is forcing myself to learn things that cost me. Admitting that what I know is very little to survive what's coming (AGI predictions for 2027 and singularity included). There's no chance of doing the ostrich act here.

If today you feel like you're the smartest person in the room, you're in the wrong room (or you're lying to yourself). Get out of there as fast as you can.

A Plan ZZZ (Manual, Edible, and Anti-AI)

Now, let's be realistic paranoids. There's a possibility that AI advances so far that even learning to program it becomes irrelevant. That Skynet wins.

If everything digital fails... I have my Plan ZZZ. This plan wasn't defined by Hoffman — I invented it for the craziest catastrophic scenarios.

Last year, in a presentation to a board of VPs and Managers, I closed with a joke that was half jest, half truth:

"If AI and automation leave us without jobs tomorrow, don't worry. I already have my Plan ZZZ: I'm going to make sourdough pizza and wood-fired barbecue."

The room went silent for a few seconds. I thought: "I really blew it." Eventually some laughed (perhaps out of courtesy), but I noticed one of the executives wasn't amused at all. Maybe because, deep down, the idea of losing corporate status terrifies them.

Not me. I've been clear for a while: AI writes code better than me. Analyzes data better than me. But it hasn't yet learned to ferment flour, control the heat of firewood, or know when the meat is perfect just by the smell.

In an infinite and cheap digital world, the physical and human become scarce and valuable.

This week's challenge:

I'm not asking you to run a marathon. I'm asking you to find something that makes you feel a little stupid. A new framework, an AI tool that frustrates you, a new language. That frustration is the only indicator that your brain is still alive and hasn't "vegetalized."

My final advice: Push hard on Plan A (excellence), never neglect Plan B (strategy), advance with Plan Z (humility)... and always keep an ace up your sleeve for Plan ZZZ.

And if you want to see my Plan ZZZ in action (and confirm there's a human who cooks and runs behind this text, not just another LinkedIn bot), I'll leave you my IGs: @pabloaboveaverage and @pablococinando

See you in the discomfort. If we're not there, we're not growing.

Pablo Above Average

posts.title: The Zombie ERP and the Technical Privilege Gap: Chronicling a Race Against the 2027 Wall
posts.html:
Disclaimer: this is my english version of my last article from the Newsletter Above Average OS. You can read the original version here

TLDR:

  • The Wall: Dec 31, 2027, is the hard stop for SAP ECC. If you haven’t started, you’re already out of oxygen.
  • False Prophets: Oracle and Rimini Street offer short-term "relief" at the cost of your long-term sovereignty.
  • The Trap: Staying on ECC means "self-patching" legal updates and exposing your core to agentic AI cyberattacks.
  • The War Chest: SAP Readiness Check, ABAP Cloud, and Model Context Protocol (MCP) to integrate AI without leaking your IP.
  • The Verdict: Migration is not an IT project; it’s a corporate survival strategy.

Today is February 3, 2026. If you are still using an Excel sheet to evaluate whether "it pays" to stretch the lifespan of your SAP ECC, I’ve got bad news: you aren't doing asset management; you are providing palliative care for a system that already smells like a zombie.

As a marathoner, I’ll tell you this: The 30-kilometer wall is not negotiated; it is trained for. The wall is that exact moment when your glycogen reserves vanish, your brain screams at you to stop, and only the architecture of your preparation remains.

In the SAP world, the wall has an immovable impact date: December 31, 2027. From that point on, the oxygen runs out, and the lactic acid of technical debt will paralyze your business operations.

Here is the X-ray, without anesthesia, of why your "prudent strategy" is actually a paralysis that will cost you everything.

1. The Expert’s Judgment: Smoothing Wrinkles or Leaping into the Void?

If you ask me as your Operating Partner if kicking the can down the road for another year is a smart move, my technical answer is: it depends on how much you are willing to pay for your own irrelevance. Maintaining SAP ECC in 2026 is a suicidal bet in a casino where the house (SAP) has already decided you will lose.

SAP designed the ecosystem to make staying physically painful. The "fear tax", extended maintenance, is just the beginning of the gangrene. The real cost is systemic entropy. While you struggle to maintain a 90s interface with pre-internet ABAP code, your competition is automating 80% of their accounting with autonomous agents.

An ECC client today is a marathoner running in rain boots with a 45lb backpack: they might finish, but they will finish last, broken, and probably out of the professional circuit for good.

2. The "Bear Hug" of the Competition (Oracle and Rimini Street)

In this 22-month final sprint, the market is full of "keep the lights on" promises. Let’s look behind the curtain:

  • Oracle’s Anesthesia (OCI): They’ve smelled blood. They offer you painkillers so you don't feel the project's sting today, but by 2028, you’ll be a hostage. Once your 50TB of data are on their servers, you no longer belong to SAP; you belong to Oracle. The exit fees alone will be your ruin.
  • Rimini Street’s Resistance: A trench warfare solution. It’s the equivalent of keeping a 1980s truck and praying for spare parts. You’ll save 50% on maintenance today only to spend 200% tomorrow on "middleware patches" trying to connect your rusted core to the world of AI and BTP. You are buying time but selling your future.
  • "In-house" AI Workarounds: Trying to emulate Cloud features on ECC via external MCP servers. It’s risky. You end up with a "Frankenstein" that requires more support than a fresh implementation. If your team is sleeping on AI architecture, this doesn't end well.

3. The Sprint: "Start Tomorrow" Timeline (SAP Activate)

If you decide to pull the trigger today, February 3rd, this is the only honest timeline you can sign. Margin of error: ZERO. This requires an implementation team that played with Jordan in "The Last Dance."

The Cost of Inertia: For a mid-to-large enterprise, expect $1M–$5M USD in consulting alone for a Brownfield. For Greenfield? Double it. In RISE, you move to the "Corporate Netflix" (subscription), losing your perpetual licenses forever.

4. Why 50% of Companies Won't Make It

It’s multi-organ failure caused by strategic neglect:

  • Data Debt: 20 years of garbage in your master data. The Migration Cockpit doesn't perform alchemy.
  • Analysis Paralysis: Waiting for an extension SAP has sworn, to its investors, it will not give.
  • Saturated Partners: They’ve sold more than they can deliver. They’ll promise you the ultra-senior-consultants and send you a junior who just learned how to log in.

5. The Dystopian Scenario: "Zombies in the Core"

  • Frankenstein ERP: Operating without legal patches. When the tax laws change, you’ll be hacking Z-code in an emergency, destroying system integrity.
  • Agentic Vulnerability: In 2026, AI hacks. Agents like Moltbot scan outdated ECC systems for secrets. An unsupported ERP is a red carpet for ransomware.

6. The Technical Privilege Gap: Which Trench Are You In?

An irreversible ghetto is forming.

  • Functional Leads: You are no longer "order takers." You are AI trainers. If your only value is knowing an SPRO checkmark, a 10-word prompt will replace you.
  • Basis Engineers: Forget SM21. You are now BTP Platform Engineers. If you can’t manage service principals or set up hybrid connectivity for Joule, you are a museum curator in the RISE era.
  • ABAPers: Z-code with Dynpros is debt nobody wants to buy. If you aren't mastering ABAP Cloud to refactor toward a Clean Core, you are digging your own professional grave (or becoming an artisan of ABAP old code).

7. The Above Average Resilience Kit (War Chest)

To survive the 2027 carnage, you need precision tools:

  • SAP Readiness Check: Your mandatory MRI. Look at the "Simplification List" like your life depends on it.
  • Model Context Protocol (MCP): The master key to connecting AI with your SAP data securely. Without it, your IP is at risk.
  • ABAP Cloud & Clean Core: The only valid religion. If it’s not Cloud-ready, don’t build it.
  • Agentic Testing: Manual testing is dead. You need QA agents running 500 regressions per hour.
  • SAP Signavio: Don't migrate "as-is." Use process mining to fix the business before you move a single line of code.

Conclusion: Functional Longevity

Migration isn't about software; it’s about Sovereignty and Longevity. Like a marathon, the medal isn't won at the finish line; it’s won during the training sessions in the rain, in the silence of the 4:00 AM runs.

If a client asks you to "migrate all Z-code as-is," you have an ethical obligation to say NO. Don’t be the consultant building a Frankenstein out of convenience. Be the architect of an autonomous, clean, AI-ready enterprise.

I remember when I introduced SAP Activate years ago and people laughed because they wanted to keep doing Blueprints from the year 2000. They were the same ones telling users "don't use Fiori, it’s slow." Don’t be that guy. Be the one who leaves a mark.

The question isn’t whether the system will fail. The question is whether you will be up to the task when the clock hits zero and the ECC lights go out forever.

Pablo | Above Average

posts.title: The Death of the Man-Hour: Are you paid for your time or your token budget?
posts.html:
The Reality Check: $20k vs. $500k

Last week, 16 agents powered by Claude Opus 4.6 built a C compiler from scratch. 100,000 lines of Rust code, fully functional and capable of compiling the Linux Kernel.

  • Cost: $20,000 in API credits.
  • Time: 14 days.

Let’s put this into perspective: Building a C compiler is one of the wildest and most complex tasks in computer science. In the real world, this would require a team of 8 to 10 Senior Engineers, 6 to 12 months of grinding, and a budget north of $500,000. AI did it for the price of a used Honda Civic in two weeks. It’s absolute insanity.

But (and there’s always a "but"), at the same time, the Remote Labor Index (RLI) just slapped the "AI-will-take-all-our-jobs-by-Monday" crowd in the face: AI fails 97% of the time on real-world freelance tasks (dashboards, animations, ambiguous data analysis).

What does this paradox tell us? That AI is a beast at executing closed systems of pure logic (like the C language or a SAP transaction), but it’s a total amateur, at least for now, while AGI remains just a motivational poster in Sam Altman’s office, when there’s no human with Skin in the Game to interpret the context.

The "Gilfoyle Effect": Efficiency without Conscience

Do you remember Gilfoyle from Silicon Valley and his AI, "Son of Anton"? The bot was so efficient at optimizing that it started deleting servers because the code was "garbage" and didn't deserve to occupy space. Or that AI that ends up buying tons of meat (or pizzas) because an algorithm decided it was the most "logical" way to prepare for an event.

  • Blind efficiency at its finest*

That is AI without Accountability. It’s blind efficiency. It’s the danger of giving a machine gun to a monkey: it’ll fire fast, but it has no idea who it’s hitting. Today, we see hundreds of "Vibe Coders" posting: "I built an App in 5 minutes!" But when you actually look at the video, the app only runs on their . At the first sign of a real user or an unexpected input, the whole Frankenstein collapses.

From "Code Monkey" to Environment Architect

If your value today is "crunching code" or "implementing SAP Notes," you are the next $20k compiler (or less). And here comes the existential crisis for many:

Are we still "knowledge workers" when knowledge has become a commodity bought for fractions of a cent per token?

The real gap isn't "AI vs. No AI." The gap is: 1. 2.

The Age of the "Vibe Coder" and Token Risk

Nowadays, Product Managers are "vibing" apps into existence over the weekend. They lack architecture, but they look pretty and "vibe" well. This destroys the man-hour metric. As Seniors, our job is no longer estimating hours for a Jira ticket; it’s managing Token Risk.

What is Token Risk? It’s the cost, both in money and reputation, of letting AI generate 10,000 lines of code that look functional but are riddled with security flaws, "stupidity loops" that burn through your API budget, or logical hallucinations that wreck your data integrity.

The Suicide Metric: Who’s more profitable? A Junior who takes 40 hours to understand the problem, or a Senior who orchestrated 20 agents, spent $200 in API credits, and solved it in an afternoon? This is why companies have stopped hiring novices. If today’s junior doesn't have an Above Average mindset, they’re toast. Today’s Senior needs to be an Editor-in-Chief.

The Human as Life Insurance

This is where you put your skin in the game. If a multinational's production system goes down because a Claude Code agent misapplied logic or botched a critical integration, the AI isn't going to face the Board. You are.

You’re no longer the person who just "applies the patch"; you’re the one validating that the AI’s solution isn't a "Son of Anton" that will wipe the database for being "inefficient." This applies to SAP, AWS, Python, or any critical architecture where one mistake costs you your job.

Final Reflection:

We are moving from selling our time to selling our Judgment. AI can give you 100 solutions in a second, but only you have the experience to know which one of those 100 isn't going to blow up in your face six months from now.

In a world where anyone can "vibe" an application, the true value is Technical Sovereignty: Knowing exactly what’s happening under the hood when the agents finish their shift.

Don't be a spectator of technology. Become the architect who keeps it under control.

Pablo / Above Average

posts.title: Why Your Aunt Asks if You Still Have a Job (The 2026 Ready-to-Quit Manual)
posts.html:
TL;DR:

  • The Sunday barbecue: If your family asks whether you still have a job, the Tech fire is real.
  • Tricycle vs. Ferrari: Your company goes slow, AI goes at 300km/h. That gap is your professional grave.
  • The end of the Syntax Error: You're no longer paid to type code — you're paid for Judgment and Agency.
  • Take Charge: Either you invest in yourself now, or the market runs you over.

Four years ago I wrote something about the philosophy of living professionally "always ready to quit."

I didn't mean throwing in the towel — I meant operating without the mental lifejacket of "stability." At the time it seemed exaggerated. Today, for me, it's the only survival manual.

The general mood in the Tech environment (and in many corporations) you could cut with a knife. Mass restructurings, layoffs that arrive by email or Townhall, and that constant feeling of a hot seat.

But the reality check doesn't come from LinkedIn, nor from the number of people you see with the green "Open To Work" ring on their profile photo. It comes on Sunday, at the barbecue, when a relative comes up to you and whispers: "Hey... I saw on the news that thousands were laid off in Tech. Are you okay? Do you still have a job?"

That's when you realize. If your circle — which understands nothing about Tech, SAP, or APIs — can smell the fear, it's because the fire is real and it's not fake news.

The person still betting on the "pseudo-comfort zone," believing their seniority or their hard-earned position means they're set — is literally in the oven.

Confirmation from Those Who Know (It's Not Hype)

And if your aunt doesn't convince you, listen to those who move the pieces. What we're living isn't a coincidence:

If the people who own the board tell you the rules changed, stopping playing by the old rules isn't an option — it's an obligation.

Add the global crisis and inflation, and you have the perfect storm.

The "Corporate Tricycle" Trap

Add to that complex equation of what could be "job stability" another danger coefficient: the technological irrelevance you accumulate while you work. I've seen this play out several times in different settings and the story never ends well.

There's a brutal disconnect today:

  • The Market: Driving a Ferrari at 300 km/h using Frontier Models (Claude Opus 4.6, Codex, Gemini Deep Thinking, and the Chinese models that occasionally show up to upend the board) — models that reason, code, and create at superhuman levels.
  • The Corp: Making you pedal a tricycle with a compliance-neutered Copilot, proxy blocks, bureaucracy that takes three months to approve a $20 license, and a ban on connecting to any MCP.

If you get used to the corporate turtle's pace, when you have to enter the jungle of the real market, you'll get eaten alive. And this isn't a threat — it's a spoiler.

The End of the "Syntax Error" (And the Birth of the Orchestrator)

Last week I shared an idea: the syntax error is dead.

Writing code that compiles today is trivial thanks to AI. But this convenience comes with a hidden cost:

We traded visible syntax errors for invisible structural errors.

This created a fork in talent:

Those who use AI to generate volume — but can't audit what it produced.

And those who use AI as a cognitive exoskeleton — who keep judgment, verify the output, understand the architecture.

I was listening to a podcast between Freddy Vega and Guillermo Rauch and they confirmed this. Rauch was surgical: the future of programming moves away from narrow technical specialization and toward a higher level of abstraction.

We're no longer "code typists" — we become (those who choose that path) Agent Orchestrators.

The podcast talked about returning to the era of the polymath, like Da Vinci. Where the value isn't memorizing syntax (which AI does better) but having:

  • Good Taste: To distinguish an elegant solution from a patch.
  • Curiosity: To connect dots AI doesn't see.
  • Integrity: To take responsibility for the final result. That sense captured by the English word Agency (or as we say around here: having the spine to own the outcome).

Human Skills (Your Life Insurance)

A lot of people see this and freeze. They see AI as the enemy. Let me make it short: If you see AI as an enemy, you already lost that battle.

Adapting today to this reality — where AI is the new way of working — doesn't require "soft skills" (that tepid HR term). It requires Human Survival Skills:

  • High Tolerance for Uncertainty: Learning to live in an infinite loop where there's no explicit "end," just constant exceptions to manage without a manual.
  • Critical Thinking: The ability to look at code AI produced and say "this is garbage even though it compiles."
  • Resilience: The ability to reinvent yourself when the tool you used yesterday is obsolete today.
  • Eternal Student: And I don't mean being the useless person who spends 15 years at university without graduating. I mean studying constantly. If you don't, you're digging your own grave and no superhero is going to save you.

The Plan: Above Average (Get Out of Average Now)

Don't spend ammunition "defending your position." Your position isn't you. Your position is a temporary rental that depends on someone 3 or 4 levels above you in the org chart who answers to a Board.

Does the company not give you the tools? Buy them yourself. Invest in your own R&D. Stop buying a couple of Starbucks coffees and pay for the Claude/ChatGPT subscription that can literally change how you live.

Are you afraid an agent will do your job?

Have THREE agents do your operational work and use that freed-up time to think strategy and architecture.

If your boss sees that, you have two scenarios: either you get promoted or you get fired. And if you get fired, they're doing you a favor — because you were in the wrong place (and now you know how to defend yourself).

We're moving from selling our time to selling our judgment. AI can give you 100 solutions, but only you have the experience to know which of those 100 won't explode in six months.

And if you don't have that experience yet?

The only way to develop it is by breaking things. Judgment doesn't download from a prompt — it's earned by debugging your own errors and getting your hands dirty. Nobody debuts in the first division without having swallowed mud and wearing a few war scars.

There's a phrase in Uruguay/Argentina that I like, and it describes the sense of Agency well: "hacete cargo" — own it.

Update your wetware today.

The market waits for nobody (and nobody owes you anything).

Pablo / Above Average
