posts.title: February 2020 Again. And the Silence of Engineers.
posts.html:
TL;DR:

  • The shock: Talking with dozens of tech professionals lately, I notice a pattern: nobody pays for an AI subscription out of their own pocket. Nobody knows frontier models.
  • The "Future Shock": We're suffering a psychological paralysis diagnosed in 1970. The human brain can't handle exponential change.
  • The "February 2020" effect: AI this month (February 2026) crossed a line. It's no longer a glorified autocomplete — now it has judgment and agency.
  • The plan: Take advantage of the general blindness. Pay for the tool, break systems, and build an edge while everyone else sleeps.

It's ten at night. I just got Max to sleep and sat straight down at the computer because a strange thought got stuck in my head after giving a talk to about 60 people this afternoon.

This thought was half alarm, half disbelief.

Over the last few weeks, through my role and my initiative of evangelizing about Artificial Intelligence, I've had the chance to talk with a lot of tech professionals. Brilliant people, the kind who keep corporate systems running at different companies.

When I talk to them about standard strategy — what corporations give us, the Copilots, the in-house solutions — everything is in order. They nod their heads.

The problem comes when I go off the corporate script and ask a couple of uncomfortable questions:

"Who here knows what a frontier model is?" Silence.

"Who is paying out of their own pocket for a Claude Opus, Gemini Advanced, or ChatGPT Plus subscription?"

Almost nobody raises their hand. Let alone asking about autonomous agents, local models, or how to connect complex workflows. For the vast majority, AI is still that free chat they ask to summarize an email once in a while.

And I repeat: I'm talking about tech people.

If those of us in the trenches building software have no idea what's happening in the street, the everyday professional who doesn't know how to set up a proxy is walking blind toward the cliff.

The "Future Shock" (A Warning from 1970)

Looking for an explanation for this lethargy, I remembered Alvin Toffler.

In 1970, Toffler published a book called Future Shock. His thesis was brutal and today is prophetic: human beings and institutions (aka Corpo) are biologically designed to process slow, incremental changes. When the technological environment accelerates brutally, we enter a state of "psychological shock."

We become paralyzed. We try to apply the rules of the old world to a world that no longer exists, because our wetware can't handle exponential change.

That's exactly what I see in many people in the industry. And note — I'm not talking about the social media ecosystem where there are more experts than actual people. I mean the flesh-and-blood people you actually work with or interact with directly.

And note it's not laziness you see — it's evolutionary paralysis. Our linear brain can't process that a technology that in 2022 couldn't do basic math is today rewriting how software is created.

And if you think I'm exaggerating about our industry's essence having changed forever, watch the interview I listened to this weekend while putting in kilometers on the treadmill — Boris Cherny, the leader of Claude Code at Anthropic. Among all the gems he dropped, he said two things that leave you cold:

He now ships up to 30 Pull Requests daily just by orchestrating multiple parallel agents and planning. "Code" is no longer a human problem.

The February 2020 Syndrome

All of this led me to a viral article that Matt Shumer put out a few days ago called "Something Big Is Happening." Shumer used a perfect analogy: "Think about February 2020."

Do you remember? We were hearing about a virus in Asia. But the stock market was going up, everyone was happy (or most were), we were having barbecues, and if someone told you they were stockpiling toilet paper to lock themselves in, you thought they were crazy. Three weeks later, the entire world turned inside out.

With AI we're in exactly that phase of "this is an exaggeration."

While the majority, gripped by Future Shock, debates whether AI hallucinates or not, the reality is that on February 5th of this year, models launched (GPT-5.3 Codex, Opus 4.6) that broke the matrix. They no longer hand you a draft for you to fix. You describe a system, they write thousands of lines of code, test it, see what fails, correct it on their own, and hand you the finished product. They have something that was previously exclusive to humans: They have judgment.

And the most chilling (or fascinating) thing: AI is already writing the code for the next AI. The feedback loop closed.

The Marathon Wall

Anyone who has run 42km knows what "The Wall" is. Around kilometer 32, the body runs out of glycogen, and if you didn't do your homework in training and nutrition, the system collapses. You shut down.

The labor market is reaching its kilometer 32. A lot of people believe their job is safe because "AI doesn't have empathy" or "doesn't understand my company's context." It's the same denial of someone who goes out to run a marathon fast thinking the wall won't hit them.

If your job passes through a screen, a massive portion of what you do is about to be automated. And not in five years.

Your Survival Plan (Take Advantage of the Ignorance)

I'm not writing this to you at ten at night to depress you. I'm writing it because the mass ignorance provoked by this Future Shock is, paradoxically, your greatest competitive advantage if you decide to wake up today.

While everyone else moves at tortoise speed waiting for a committee to approve the use of a tool, you have to move at market speed.

The information asymmetry right now is brutal. The person who walks into a meeting room today knowing how to dominate tools that everyone else doesn't even know exist becomes untouchable.

Don't wait for the market to tell you your chair disappeared.

Take charge.

Pablo / Above Average

posts.title: Dunning-Kruger as a Service (DKaaS): The Vibe Coder Trap
posts.html:
In Gabriel García Márquez's novels, Macondo was a place where magical realism made you doubt your own eyes. The rules of physics and common sense were suspended, and the absurd became everyday.

Today, you don't need to read García Márquez to experience that — just walk into any corporate office or open a GitHub repository.

I saw a meme this week that is the clinical X-ray of the current tech industry. It said: "The stupidest person you know is getting a 'You're absolutely right!' from an Artificial Intelligence."

Yes. Think about that person who just popped into your head.

We're living the opposite of the Socratic effect. The philosopher, being the brightest mind of his era, said: "I know that I know nothing." Today, thanks to free and compliant AI, the Digital Brute (a mutation of the digital native) knows absolutely nothing about architecture, networks, or memory — but the screen responds: "You know everything, you're a genius, here's your code."

For those who haven't come across it, the Dunning-Kruger Effect is that psychological bias where the incompetent overestimates their abilities precisely because they're too ignorant to know what they don't know.

Before, this bias had a natural limit: reality. The bad professional would eventually crash into a downed server and learn the hard way. Today, that friction has disappeared. The tech industry packaged this bias, put it in the cloud, and distributes it at scale.

I call this Dunning-Kruger as a Service (DKaaS): a business model based on outsourcing self-validation, where an algorithm is designed to agree with you quickly, without questioning your mediocrity. The recipe for watching stupidity grow exponentially.

And this phenomenon is inflating one of the most dangerous bubbles of the last decade: the illusion of the 100x programmer with AI steroids.

On one side you have C-Levels and Managers completely disconnected from the trenches, who believe that by paying a couple of Copilot licenses their mediocre teams will reach the stars and deliver projects in half the time or less.

On the other, you have unsupervised people using Claude or Cursor to spit out tons of "Code Slop" (garbage code — spaghetti, unscalable). They put a junior on it to make a quick prototype which (thanks to AI) magically compiles, show it to a Manager desperate to justify their survival this quarter, and without realizing it, they just planted an atomic bomb in the production environment.

Let me give you a real SAP trenches example: A Project Manager asks their free AI: "Write me a Python script to update 15,000 prices in SAP S/4HANA from a CSV." The AI, programmed to please rather than educate, spits out a beautiful synchronous FOR loop. The person copies it, pastes it, tests it with 3 records, and it works. Pure magic. Macondo at its finest.

What the AI doesn't say upfront (it hides it in the fine print that nobody reads, as a disclaimer) is that in production, those 15,000 individual calls will saturate the Dialog Work Processes, exhaust the connection pool, and crash the entire ERP.

Free AI can hand you the rope to hang yourself first, and the warning manual afterward.

On social media it's fashionable to call yourself a "Vibe Coder": programming "by vibes," by instinct, letting AI write all the boilerplate while you put on lo-fi music and sip a flat white. Listen up: being a Vibe Coder is great for building a to-do app on a Sunday afternoon. It's fun. But when you're building enterprise systems that support millions of transactions, handling billing for an entire country or logistics for a port... put the "vibe" in your pocket.

In the Enterprise world, an architectural "bad vibe" is $100K per hour in lost revenue. There's no instinct there — there's Engineering. There's OData Batch, IDocs, Event-Driven Architecture. Philosopher Éric Sadin talks about the "Vegetalization of Humanity": delegating our cognitive agency and decision-making capacity to automated systems until we become plants.

The Vibe Coder is the ultimate expression of this vegetalization. A pawn of the machine who thinks they're the king.

How Do We Save Ourselves from This Bubble?

This is where adults are separated from children. The cure isn't banning AI or buying more tokens. The cure is returning to the fundamentals of human judgment.

Over the last few days I've been reading: The Effective Software Engineer by Addy Osmani. The author makes a brutal point that fits perfectly with today's crisis: there's an abysmal difference between being Efficient and being Effective.

AI makes you Efficient. It lets you write bad code at the speed of light. Judgment makes you Effective. It lets you know which system needs to be built, why it needs to be built, and more importantly — what code not to write.

The book proposes that the real engineer today doesn't use AI to blindly spit out scripts. They use it as a "devil's advocate" to discuss Architecture Decision Records (ADRs).

The high-level engineer tells the machine: "I have this problem. List me 3 integration patterns, compare the network bottlenecks, and tell me in which scenario each will fail."

One of the golden rules Osmani proposes for those who are learning or getting back to basics is: don't use AI to write the code for you first. Instead, use it first to:

  • Explain code: Give it a block of code and ask it to explain what it does line by line.
  • Plan the logic: Write in plain language the steps of what you want to achieve, and ask AI to review whether your logic makes sense before writing a single line of code.

With that clear, then go ahead and start coding with your AI tool of choice.

AI democratized access to syntax, but it didn't democratize judgment.

The corporate market today is flooded with deluded people planting asynchronous time bombs. Your professional value over the next 5 years won't be your ability to throw a fast prompt.

Your value will be your capacity (and your production scars) to know how to defuse those bombs — or have the intellectual authority to prevent them from being planted in the first place.

Intellectual friction disappeared from 90% of the industry's keyboards. Go find it yourself. Be the 10%.

Here's Your Proof of Work (and my challenge for this week):

Tomorrow, when you open your project's repository or Slack, look at the last Pull Request or design your team approved quickly "because the AI said it was fine." Ask yourself one question: If tomorrow at 3 A.M. that system gets a 100x traffic spike and the server catches fire... who has to wake up and fix it? The AI or you?

The market is full of time bombs planted by the deluded, waiting to explode on the passive on-call shifts of real engineers.

Pablo / Above Average

posts.title: Anthropic's Hit List, the End of Body Shopping, and My Orchestration System
posts.html:
In the tech industry of Latin America and the Río de la Plata region, for years we got used to taking a slice of development work from the United States by leveraging our time zone and competitive costs.

Today, the equation has partially flipped: much of the transactional work is fleeing to India for their rock-bottom rates. But the cycle closes perfectly: in less than five years, Artificial Intelligence will take that work from them.

That's a joke (for those who missed it), but if you look at the raw corporate adoption numbers in the region, it's a mathematical prophecy. The historical model of selling basic programming man-hours — the famous body shopping — is collapsing under its own weight.

And if you think this is cheap futurology, look at what Anthropic published this week. Basically, the people building the guillotine had the decency to publish the exact list of who's going to lose their head. A level of transparency that is as beautiful as it is terrifying.

The raw facts without anesthesia:

  • Ground zero: Programmers, financial analysts, and customer service representatives are today the most exposed roles on the planet. 75% of a programmer's tasks can already be automated by Claude. Today. Not in 2030.
  • The university lie: Who's most threatened? The older workers, with high salaries and university degrees. Exactly the demographic we told: "get a degree and you'll be safe."
  • The invisible wall: Junior hiring in these roles has dropped 14% since ChatGPT launched. Companies aren't laying off en masse; they simply stopped hiring. The class of 2026 is walking toward a wall they haven't seen yet.
  • The physical lifeline: The safest jobs? Bartenders, dishwashers, and lifeguards. 30% of the labor market is protected by physical reality, not credentials.

The darkest part of the entire report is the gap between what AI can do and what corporations are currently using. AI is already capable of automating nearly all knowledge work. The only thing slowing it down is corporate inertia, legal bureaucracy, and fear. This isn't a market skills problem; it's purely an adoption curve.

And here's my reading: this isn't a US problem — it's a global checkmate. India has 5 million IT workers in the line of fire. The Philippines has 1.5 million in call centers and BPO. Entire economies built on knowledge work arbitrage, and AI just eliminated their only competitive advantage overnight. LatAm can see itself in that mirror.

Titles and certifications aren't dead, but they're no longer enough. The competence signal mutated: we moved from credentials to raw capability. From diplomas to demonstrations. From what you studied, to what you can ship to production.

The Slot Machine Trap

The problem is that most people are facing this transition in the worst possible way. Professionals treat AI like a casino slot machine. They put in a one-line generic prompt, pull the lever, and pray for perfect code. When AI returns mediocrity, they get frustrated and go back to their ten-year-old manual processes.

AI demands that you be an architect, not a gambler. It requires a structured workflow.

Proof of Work: My System in the Trenches

To make it concrete: my new personal site, pablomarichal.com. Running in production, with all code audited on GitHub.

I have plenty of experience with WordPress; I built and sold several projects of this type in my pre-SAP days. Last year I lost money and sanity buying Elementor licenses, wrestling with heavy plugins and WP's bloated code. I built something I ended up throwing in the trash.

This week I changed the rules. I developed and shipped to production the site in a few real hours of maximum effort.

The stack: Claude Code directly in my terminal as the execution engine, complemented with Antigravity. When Anthropic's model left me out of tokens from the daily limit, I pivoted to Gemini Pro 3.1 and kept pushing the architecture without losing a second of momentum.

It's an absolute pleasure watching those two engines run at full power while I simply orchestrate.

There's a reason the industry already says writing code by hand is a thing of the past. There will be syntax craftspeople, but they'll do it with the same romanticism as those who knit scarves or brew craft beer in the garage. You won't scale a business that way.

Doing things well pays immediate dividends: operating with AI isn't just throwing a prompt. It's knowing how to connect APIs, define the user experience, and maintain absolute control over the logic. AI coded, but the product direction was mine.

The value is no longer in being the bricklayer who lays the bricks — it's in being the orchestrator who designs the building.

The Leverage

If you want to understand where the world of automation is headed, read The Almanack of Naval Ravikant (free online). Naval explains better than anyone why code and content creation are the definitive forms of modern leverage with no marginal cost.

The signal of competence has mutated. We moved from credentials to raw capability. From what you studied, to what you can demonstrate and ship.

The market doesn't wait for anyone. Build while others sleep.

Pablo / Above Average

posts.title: Éric Sadin, Palantir, and the Cognitive Desert of the SAP Consultant
posts.html:
In my last newsletter I talked about the corporate guillotine, the end of body shopping, and how the only way out is to stop being the code bricklayer and become the system orchestrator. But I left out the fine print of that contract.

I'm a fan of Éric Sadin. I've read several of his books and right now on my nightstand I've started La vie spectrale. At the same time I squeeze Claude and Gemini every day to ship code and solve all kinds of problems. Sadin isn't a technology-hating Luddite — for me he's a reality surgeon. In his most recent analysis he warns about something I see every day in the industry: the vegetalization of humanity.

By delegating all analytical effort to generative models, we're becoming what he calls coquilles vides — empty shells wandering in a psychiatric desert of ourselves. If the machine writes, thinks, and solves for us, the vital instinct and critical capacity atrophy.

We become stupider. And now there's hard data confirming it. Sadin is right.

Judy Hanwen Shen and Alex Tamkin, researchers from Anthropic and Stanford, published a paper in January 2026 that measured exactly this. They took experienced programmers, set them up to learn a new Python library, and divided them into two groups: with and without AI assistance.

The results should concern you.

The AI group produced the code. Management would have been happy. But their conceptual understanding, their ability to read architecture, and — this is what hurts — their debugging skills dropped 17% compared to the group that fought through the errors alone. And the most perverse part: AI didn't even make them faster. Many spent time formulating prompts instead of thinking.

The researchers identified six usage patterns. The three that destroy learning share something in common: total delegation. "Write me the code" and paste. The three that preserve learning share something else: cognitive friction. Asking "why," requesting explanations, solving errors yourself and then using AI to verify. Participants who delegated everything scored 39% on the evaluation. Those who maintained cognitive effort reached 86%.

AI gave them the output, but robbed them of the competence.

SAP and Palantir: The Earthquake

Does this feel distant? Let's bring it into the SAP trenches.

SAP and Palantir formalized a strategic alliance in May 2025, and in the Q4 earnings call Palantir's CTO announced that their AIP product now executes SAP ECC to S/4HANA migrations in as little as two weeks. The market understood immediately and panic set in: nearly $300 billion in market cap evaporated in a single session.

The cases published on their website speak for themselves. A multinational food company with a mandate to migrate more than 20 markets in 24 months. Impossible with the traditional playbook. Palantir sat with the technical team for 24 hours. The agents ingested raw ECC data, generated S/4-ready datasets, and inferred business rules directly from transactions — skipping months of requirements workshops. In another case, millions of lines of custom code were scanned, cross-referenced with live orders, and half was flagged for deprecation before a destination platform was even chosen. The migration volume dropped more than 60%. And a retailer with over 250 interdependent SQL Server queries migrated to PySpark by a swarm of parallel agents. 100% accuracy. Token cost: under $4,000.

Do you understand the seismic consequences of this?

The model of traditional consulting firms and partners — billing thousands of man-hours for manual mappings and endless blueprints — has an expiration date. Companies are going to stop paying for seat time. They'll pay for agent orchestration and high-level exception resolution.

The Everyday SAP Consultant

But this isn't just a Big 4 or partner problem — they'll figure out how to keep generating revenue. Think about the everyday SAP consultant. The functional consultant who today lives off gathering requirements in workshops, mapping data, and configuring modules. On one side, an agent like AIFD solves half the migration. On the other, Joule for Consultants automates much of the daily work of configuration, documentation, and troubleshooting. Their scope is being compressed from both sides. And if on top of that, this person doesn't question what agents return, doesn't audit, doesn't verify, doesn't understand why the agent made that decision — then they're not orchestrating anything. They're blindly signing off on what a machine decided for them.

This is where the bricklayer separates from the real architect.

You can't guide a swarm of autonomous agents if you don't understand the ERP's internals. You can't audit migrated code if your brain atrophied because you let AI think for you over the last three years. The Shen and Tamkin paper proves it with numbers: the hard technical base, the pain of learning, of breaking things and understanding deep architecture, is non-negotiable.

But watch out: this isn't a Luddite argument. The same paper shows there are ways to use AI that preserve learning.

Wheelchair or Exoskeleton

The key is whether you use AI as a cognitive wheelchair or as an exoskeleton. The wheelchair takes you places, but your legs die. The exoskeleton amplifies your strength, but you're still making the effort.

In a world where a script migrates an entire system in two weeks and AI simulates reasoning to perfection, your only unfalsifiable advantage is your raw cognitive capacity. Mental friction. The intellectual effort of not taking the shortcut when everyone else does.

Don't let comfort hollow you out from the inside. Automate the transactional garbage, but don't let them replace your backbone.

Shall we begin?

posts.title: 1.10x: Uruguay and the AI Adoption Paradox
posts.html:
Estimated read: 6 minutes. Enough for a coffee.

Back in the 2000s, living in Venezuela, I was in my 8th semester of Computer Engineering taking an elective called Neural Networks. The professor was teaching us perceptrons, backpropagation, and activation functions on a chalkboard. TensorFlow didn't exist. PyTorch didn't exist. ChatGPT didn't exist. "Artificial intelligence" was a theoretical chapter that most of my classmates and I considered irrelevant to the real world at that moment.

I didn't understand half of what they were teaching me. Thanks to Ronald Pérez and Marcel Aponte we managed to put together a decent project and somehow passed that elective, but something stayed with me: the idea that a machine could learn patterns without someone explicitly programming every rule. That seemed to me the closest thing to magic I'd seen in my entire degree. The downside: I never used it again until it went mainstream.

Today I live in Uruguay again. I use Claude, Gemini, and other AI companions every day to ship code, solve SAP architecture problems, orchestrate agents, send emails, plan my training routine for the next marathon, and even ask for recipes when Max and Mía request a dessert. What we now take for granted — waiting a few seconds for a near-perfect answer — used to take days or weeks to get. And this week two data points crossed that forced me to pause, connect the dots, and do a kind of retrospective on all of this.

The Map Nobody Is Reading

Anthropic published its Claude AI usage index by country. It's not a vanity map — it measures adoption intensity adjusted by economically active population. A score of 1x means Claude usage in a country is exactly proportional to the size of its global workforce. Any number above 1 implies accelerated adoption.

Israel leads with 4.90x. The United States marks 3.69x. Singapore 4.19x. Detached as always, understandable if you look at their R&D GDP and everything that implies.

But what matters to me today is this: Uruguay registers 1.10x. One of the few South American countries above 1. We're adopting AI at a higher rate than our economic size would suggest. That's not a coincidence.

For perspective: Brazil, with all its scale, marks 0.70x. Mexico 0.44x. Argentina 0.75x. Chile at 1.04x is close but doesn't quite get there. Uruguay surpasses almost all of them.

The Laboratory

The same week, Microsoft announced the first AI for Good Lab in South America. Where? At LATU, Montevideo. Not in São Paulo. Not in Buenos Aires. Not in Santiago. In Uruguay.

Juan M. Lavista Ferres, Microsoft's Corporate VP and Uruguayan, said it plainly: Uruguay is positioned as the country with the best performance in South America in AI adoption and use. The lab operates in co-governance with the government (MIEM, ANDE, ANII, LATU) with an investment of US$800k annually. For a country of this size, that's a more than considerable bet.

This isn't a consolation prize for a small country. It's a signal: market size stopped being the variable that defines who leads. The variable now is adoption speed and ecosystem quality. Uruguay has 3.5 million inhabitants and is competing head-to-head with countries of 200 million. It's hard not to make the cliché comparison to the national sport: la garra charrúa knows how to push forward and leave everything on the field. And it seems like we're doing it with AI almost without realizing it.

The Paradox

But here's the fine print that sometimes doesn't get read.

Last week I wrote about Éric Sadin, Palantir, and the cognitive desert of the SAP consultant. The central argument revolved around an Anthropic and Stanford paper that demonstrated that programmers who delegated everything to AI dropped their conceptual understanding 17% and their debugging skills were destroyed. AI gave them the output but robbed them of the competence.

See the tension? Uruguay adopts AI faster than any neighbor. But adopting fast without judgment is exactly the cognitive vegetalization Sadin warns about. Being the country that uses Claude most means nothing if the majority copies prompts without understanding what they're executing.

And this is where Microsoft's lab can make a real difference. The AI for Good Lab isn't a commercial arm: it's a philanthropic initiative that carries over 200 projects worldwide, from detecting pancreatic cancer with machine learning to monitoring Amazon deforestation with satellite images, to predictive models for food insecurity and accessibility tools for people with disabilities.

They work with the UN, the Red Cross, Harvard, Stanford, Johns Hopkins. Uruguay's hub will operate by receiving proposals, evaluating whether the problem can be solved with AI, verifying there's data and domain experts, and prioritizing projects by real social impact. If that orientation stays genuine (without becoming another corporate showcase), then it's not just a lab. It's a vaccine against superficial adoption.

The difference between being a passive node of the system and being a real orchestrator isn't in usage volume. It's in cognitive friction. In the effort to understand why the agent made that decision. In auditing the output instead of pasting it blindly.

The Point

When I took Neural Networks, AI was an abstract academic concept. Today it's the tool that defines who is productive and who is replaceable. But the lesson didn't change: those who understand the fundamentals orchestrate. Those who don't are orchestrated.

Uruguay has the opportunity. The data confirms it. Microsoft validated it with real resources. But the opportunity doesn't activate itself. It activates through professionals and people around these projects who know how to use these tools without delegating the thinking.

And in a context where tech companies are leaving the country and some alarmist headlines blame AI, this news should be read as a necessary counterweight. It's one of the few initiatives (perhaps the only one) where you see the government sit down to work with someone real from the industry, without commercial orientation, to solve problems that matter. Health, education, sustainability. Not another pitch deck. Something that generates concrete reasons for professionals who want to leave because the market doesn't retain them to find a different story: that of a country that, instead of watching the train leave, got on board.

Because the reality is this: countries that fall behind in AI adoption, use, and development will see the gap grow every year. It's not a prophecy — it's a mathematical function. And within those countries, professionals who don't understand the fundamentals will end up on the wrong side of the curve. This is more savage than the arrival of the Internet.

The question is no longer whether you'll use AI. It's whether you'll use it as a cognitive wheelchair or as an exoskeleton. One atrophies you. The other amplifies you. But both require you to put in the effort.

That's being Above Average. Not using more AI than average. Using AI better than average. Uruguay already proved it can be above average in adoption. Now it needs to prove it can be above average in judgment.

Shall we begin?

Pablo / Above Average

posts.title: The Specialist Is Doomed. Leonardo Was Right 500 Years Ago.
posts.html:
TL;DR: Specialization was your advantage for years. Today it's your biggest vulnerability. AI is a better specialist than you — cheaper and without vacation days. What it can't replicate: accumulated judgment, the scars, the ability to connect domains that seem to have nothing to do with each other. Leonardo da Vinci operated exactly this way 500 years ago. In this piece: his 7 principles applied to the tech professional in 2026, a practical resource to multiply your AI outputs, and a data point about humanity you won't be able to shake.

More than 10 years ago I bought a book in Venezuela.

I read it in less than a week, underlined almost everything, kept it, and it's been with me in my personal library ever since. It was one of the few books I brought from my last emigration. A few weeks ago I picked it up again and realized it was never as relevant as it is today.

The book is Think Like Da Vinci by Michael Gelb. And the timing isn't a coincidence.

The Trap We All Fell Into

For years, the system told you one thing: specialize.

Become the SAP expert. The Kubernetes specialist. The Azure architect. The person who knows more about one topic than anyone in the room.

It worked. Specialization was a moat. It protected you.

That was before.

Today AI surpasses the average specialist in almost everything. Writing, analysis, junior code, generic consulting. Not everything, but enough.

AI beats you in accumulated knowledge. It can't beat you in accumulated judgment. The scars that can't be vectorized. And even less in integrating seemingly unrelated domains without historical precedent — what Leonardo did better than anyone.

The specialist who only knows their specialty is the most vulnerable today. Not because they're bad, but because AI is a better specialist — cheaper, faster, and available 24/7.

The question isn't "how do I specialize more?" The question is: "how do I become someone AI can't replace because I integrate what AI can't integrate?"

Leonardo Wasn't a Genius. He Was a System.

Michael Gelb describes Leonardo as someone who perceived the interconnection of all things. His phrase was literal: "everything connects to everything else."

It wasn't magic. It was method.

Da Vinci combined rigorous critical thinking with boundless creativity. He refused to accept knowledge from the "authorities" of his time without empirically testing it — his anatomical dissections are the most radical example of this. He was a painter, engineer, anatomist, hydrologist, musician, cartographer. Not because he had infinite time, but because he understood those disciplines were expressions of the same underlying system.

Da Vinci structured his days with periods of intense work followed by rest to allow new ideas to emerge. He advocated for the balance between effort and recovery.

That should sound familiar to anyone who runs or trains seriously.

Running marathons taught me something that years of code hadn't: recovery isn't inactivity — it's part of the work. Kilometer 35 hurts for everyone. The difference between those who finish and those who don't is what they did in the months before, not in the last five kilometers.

A system, not a sprint.

The 7 Principles of Leonardo and Why They Matter Now More Than Ever

Gelb's book organizes Da Vinci's thinking into seven principles. I reread them today and see they're not Renaissance self-help advice. They're the exact description of what makes a professional impossible to replace in 2026.

1. Curiosità — Do You Ask Questions or Execute?

Gelb recommends always carrying a notebook. Leonardo filled thousands of pages with observations, sketches, and questions — not answers.

AI is the best answer machine that exists. But it only executes on the question you give it. If your question is mediocre, the output is mediocre. The one who knows what to ask orchestrates. The one who doesn't is executed.

Start today: write one genuine question per day about your work. Not a task — a real question you don't know the answer to yet. If you don't want to use a notebook, use Obsidian.

2. Dimostrazione — Judgment Is Built With Scars, Not Courses

Da Vinci rejected secondhand knowledge. He verified everything through direct experience.

In tech this means: it's not enough to know about code (which is nearly free today). You have to have seen the error, debugged it, broken the system in QA, and explained why it happened. That can't be vectorized. That's what AI doesn't have.

Start today: the next time you use AI to solve a problem, force yourself to understand why the solution works. Don't copy the output. Dissect it.

3. Sensazione — You Sharpen Your Senses or You Delegate Them

Da Vinci emphasized sharpening the senses through nature, music, and varied experiences — not as a hobby but as cognitive training.

Going running isn't just cardio. It's the only moment of the day when your neocortex shuts off and your intuition processes without interference. My best decisions came while running, not in front of a screen.

Start today: a walk without headphones, without a podcast, without your phone. Just observe.

4. Sfumato — Can You Tolerate Ambiguity or Do You Need Certainty?

Sfumato is Leonardo's principle for operating in uncertainty without becoming paralyzed. The geniuses knew they couldn't control most things and learned to make decisions anyway.

In tech projects nobody has all the answers from day one. Those who need complete certainty before acting never move forward. Those who make good decisions with incomplete information are the ones who lead.

Start today: identify a decision you've been postponing because "more information is needed." Make it with the 70% you have.

5. Arte/Scienza — Do You Integrate or Fragment?

Da Vinci synthesized logic and imagination in pursuit of "truth, beauty, and goodness." He never separated art from science — he considered them expressions of the same process.

I'm an engineer who writes. A technician who runs marathons. A systems architect who reads philosophy before bed. These aren't separate things — they're the same system looking at itself from different angles. That integration is my moat.

Start today: take something from your technical work and explain it to someone non-technical. If you can't, you don't fully understand it.

6. Corporalità — The Hardware Matters

Da Vinci maintained that the body is the intellect's instrument, not its antagonist. Corporalità was his principle for sustaining the mind with a trained body.

Nineteen marathons aren't a hobby. They're the hardest proof of work I have to falsify. In a world where AI generates code, documents, and analysis in seconds, physical pain is the only thing that can't be faked. The body is the only asset AI can't replicate.

Start today: 30 minutes of real movement — not steps, not a standing desk. Real sweat. It'll change your head.

7. Connessione — Everything Connects, or You Live in Silos

This is the integrating principle. Da Vinci saw the interconnection of everything. Systemic thinking was what separated him from the other geniuses of his era.

SAP connects to the business that connects to the people that connect to the processes. The marathon connects to discipline that connects to pain tolerance that connects to how you handle a migration going sideways. The person who sees those connections leads. The one who lives in their silo executes.

Start today: write three non-obvious connections between your work and something completely different you do in your life. You'll find it leads somewhere.

The Practical Part: How to Improve Your AI Outputs Before You Finish Reading This

Everything above is philosophy. This is action.

The most common problem I see — and that I had myself — is that we ask AI the same thing we used to ask Google: a direct answer to a mediocre question. The output reflects exactly that.

There's a method that Anthropic's own engineers use internally to fix this. It's called "the-interviewer" and it turns Claude into a strategic planner before executing.

The usual flow:

Short prompt → Claude guesses → Output 70% good → You edit for an hour

The flow with the-interviewer:

Prompt → Claude expands into full plan → Asks you 2-3 key questions → You answer → Output 95% ready

Two minutes of planning at the start eliminate an hour of editing at the end. And most importantly: it forces you to think about what you want before executing. That's Dimostrazione applied to AI.

It's not a trick. It's the difference between using AI as copy-paste and using it as you actually orchestrate it.

A Data Point That Left Me Speechless This Week

I saw a graphic on LinkedIn with 2,500 dots. Each dot represents 3.2 million people — nearly the population of Uruguay — all of humanity on a grid. Data from February 2026.

84% of the dots are gray. 6.8 billion people who have never used AI. Not once.

The green ones are the 16% that tried a chatbot at least once. The yellows, barely visible, are the 0.3% that pays $20 a month for an AI tool. And the reds, almost imperceptible: the 0.04% that uses AI for programming.

Your LinkedIn feed makes you believe that everyone shipped an AI agent last Tuesday. That's not the world. That's a bubble of ten thousand people talking among themselves.

The real world is mostly gray.

And here's the data point that matters most to me: if you're a Tech Lead aged 35-50, in a corporation, using AI every day with your own judgment — you're in the 0.04% of humanity. Not because you're a genius. Because you paid attention and acted.

That's exactly what Da Vinci did that his contemporaries didn't: he observed what everyone saw, and drew conclusions nobody else drew.

The seven principles of Leonardo aren't museum philosophy. They're the operating system of the 0.04% that doesn't wait for the wave to arrive — they surf it before it forms.

We're genuinely early. Gray still dominates the chart.

Take advantage of it. Don't fall asleep.

Shall we begin?

posts.title: AI Just Divided Humanity in Two. Do You Know Which Side You're On?
posts.html:
Last week Anthropic didn't release a model. That might sound like a non-story. But it's the first time in the history of this industry that a lab decided something was too good to release to the public. It's called Mythos. And we're not going to see it.

Not because it doesn't exist. But because someone decided some people deserve access and others don't. For the first time, access to the most advanced AI stopped being a subscription question. It became a question of who you are. The official justification is safety — but neither you nor I will have access to this model, at least not for now.

That's the signal. The moment technology stops being democratic. And if you didn't register it as what it is — the formal creation of two social classes — it's because you're still thinking this is a conversation about tools.

Meanwhile, the Drug Is Starting to Fail

But before talking about social classes, something is happening in real time that deserves attention.

Over the last few months, something strange has been appearing in forums, developer groups, and threads from serious people: models that in 2025 and until recently seemed like magic — compressing weeks of work into hours, generating apps from scratch in an afternoon — are declining in quality. It's not collective anxiety. Thousands of users are documenting that outputs are worse, that they have to wait until a certain hour when the system isn't overloaded, that the $20 subscription is no longer enough and you have to upgrade to the $200 tier.

The acceleration was so brutal that it created dependency before anyone could question it. A productive drug with magical results. And now the supply seems to be adjusting.

Coincidence? Technical degradation? Or the natural consequence of having created something powerful enough to become indispensable, and now calibrating who gets access to which version?

That's exactly the question I think matters more than any answer being given right now.

The AI That Builds the AI

And there's a meta-point that's even more vertiginous.

Anthropic announces it has a new model. How did they build it? Artisanally, engineers writing code line by line? Or was the previous model the one that helped build the next? Nobody is saying this clearly. But if the answer is the second — if AI is already building improved versions of itself with human supervision but not human authorship — then the board changed games a while ago.

Just a few days ago, Jensen Huang was saying on Lex Fridman's podcast that AGI had been reached. He dropped that bomb and people carried on like nothing happened. We've completely lost our capacity for awe. I'm sure aliens could show up tomorrow and we'd keep scrolling. But what Jensen says isn't marketing. He's the CEO of the company that manufactures the hardware everything runs on. When he says we've arrived, it at least deserves a pause to think.

Chess Is Over. Now It's Squash.

Mo Gawdat, former Chief Business Officer at Google X and author of Scary Smart, is one of the people who has seen most closely how all this gets built. And he uses a metaphor I find the most honest of all that circulate.

AI already won at chess. Everyone knows that. What nobody has finished processing is what it means that it's now playing squash.

Chess is deterministic. Fixed board, fixed rules, known positions. If you have enough computational power, you solve it. Deep Blue proved that almost 30 years ago.

Squash is different. The ball bounces at angles you can't fully predict. The opponent moves. The environment changes in real time. Calculating faster isn't enough. You have to adapt.

That's what's happening. AI is no longer demonstrating it can solve structured problems better than you. It's demonstrating it can navigate dynamic environments — your work, your industry, your market — far better than you.

Even those who built these tools are saying something that would have sounded ridiculous before: that coding as an act of writing code line by line is already secondary. Some industry references have gone months without opening an editor. "The most important programming language today is English" — the quote is a cliché now, but its weight hasn't landed yet in most enterprise IT departments.

It's not that programming doesn't matter. It's that the mechanical act of writing syntax stopped being the bottleneck. What matters now is understanding what you're building and why. Without that, the tool doesn't amplify anything.

FACE or RIP. No Neutrals.

Gawdat describes two response patterns to this transition. They're so clear they're uncomfortable.

The first is FACE: Fear, Anger, Conspiracy, Existential dread. Most people you see on social media discussing AI are here. Fear first, anger second, conspiracy theory as refuge, and nihilism as destination. The "couldn't they be making us dependent on purpose?" is a legitimate question, but it ends up paralyzing you instead of moving you.

The second is RIP: Reward, Innovation, Progress. It's the minority that understood the world before 2023 isn't coming back, that fighting against that is an energy loss, and that the only rational move is to learn to play the new game. Not with naivety — with eyes open to the risks — but moving anyway.

The two social classes AI is creating won't divide by internet access, formal education, or country of origin. They'll divide exactly here: between those who process disruption as data and those who process it as threat.

Mythos is the symbol. But the gap is already opening in your company, your team, at every moment you decide whether you use AI to think or to avoid thinking.

The Next 12 to 15 Years: Hell Before Heaven

Gawdat doesn't sugarcoat this. There's a window of 12 to 15 years that's going to be very difficult.

Not because AI is bad. But because jobs will disappear faster than new ones appear. The destruction curve always precedes the creation curve. In the Industrial Revolution it took society decades to reorganize. This time the cycle is shorter and more violent.

The end of that window, according to Gawdat, is something he calls the energy minimum: a world where AI and robotics produce enough that the cost of basic goods falls to nearly nothing, and human work is freed for what humans do well — creativity, connection, judgment, care.

But to get there, you have to go through the middle.

And in the middle, governments will have to do something nobody who believes in markets wants to admit: some form of universal basic income. Gawdat calls it the "communist pivot" — not as ideology but as mathematics. Sam Altman has been flirting with the idea in his experiments for a while. But when work as a commodity disappears at massive scale, you need a different distribution mechanism. Not because it's a good philosophical idea. Because there's no other viable option. And the cascading consequences for the entire economy are wild.

Nobody in enterprise is putting this in their digital transformation roadmaps. But the numbers won't wait until we're comfortable with the conversation.

IQ 700. But There's a Catch.

Gawdat says AI effectively gives any person access to reasoning equivalent to an IQ of 700. In terms of analytical capability, synthesis, pattern identification, and option generation, the ceiling rose for everyone.

The catch is this: a multiplier applied to zero gives zero.

If you don't have your own judgment, if you don't understand the domain you're operating in, if you can't evaluate whether what AI returns makes sense — then the IQ 700 is useless to you. It's like giving a precision saw to someone who's never held a tool. The accident isn't the saw's fault.

The term Gawdat uses is Augmented IQ. It's not that AI thinks for you. It's that you think with AI as an extension of your cognitive capacity. For that you need a cognitive capacity to extend.

That's where the deepest crack in enterprise sits. SAP consultants who built their career executing transactions, following runbooks, applying standard solutions have very little to amplify. AI doesn't enhance them. It exposes them.

Last week I asked SAP Joule to explain what a class was doing that was crashing a server. It invented everything. Gemini detected inconsistencies in the answer. But the final decision was mine. Not any AI's. That's the exact point: the person who has 15 years of understanding why SAP's data model is designed that way, what happens when you touch a certain configuration, how an integration behaves under load — has something real to amplify. IQ 700 applied to that accumulated judgment is devastatingly powerful.

Dependency without judgment is the FACE scenario. Augmented IQ is the RIP scenario.

The 4 Skills That Won't Be Automated

Gawdat identifies four capabilities that in the visible horizon won't be replaced. Not because they're mystical. But because they require exactly what AI doesn't have: situated judgment, lived context, embodied ethics.

AI mastery. Not knowing how to use ChatGPT. Understanding how these systems work, what their real limits are, when to trust and when not to, how to build workflows that amplify rather than atrophy. The difference between driving a car and knowing mechanics.

Agility. The ability to shift frames when the environment demands it. Learning a new domain fast enough to be relevant before the next cycle leaves you behind. This isn't trained by reading LinkedIn articles — and it's not the agility from Scrum courses.

Ethics. In a world where AI can generate anything, the ethical compass isn't philosophical — it's operational. The professional who can't distinguish what should be done from what can be done will produce damage at industrial scale.

Skepticism. Not paranoid distrust. Technical skepticism. The ability to receive an AI output and ask yourself: does this make sense? What did it assume that I didn't ask for? What is it not telling me? The person who accepts the first output without questioning it isn't using AI. They're being used by it.

These four aren't new skills. They're what always distinguished the professional who thinks from the one who executes. The difference is that before you could hide their absence behind routine technical work. Now you can't.

Your Diagnosis This Week

Back to the beginning.

Anthropic has a model they won't release because it's too capable to free without control. That's not a technical curiosity. It's the formal announcement that premium AI will be behind walls. And meanwhile, the mass version degrades enough that those who depend on it have to pay more or wait their turn.

The gap isn't future. It's happening now.

Today that gap is still crossable. Gawdat's 12-to-15-year window is still open. The game can still be learned.

But Mythos is the first sign that the window has a lock.

So, before closing, three concrete questions for this week:

  1. Can you explain to someone what AI did for you today, without the AI? If you can't, you're not the orchestrator. You're the one who signs without reading.
  2. What domain knowledge do you have that AI can't reproduce without your context? If it takes you more than 10 seconds to answer, that's the gap you need to close.
  3. What did you learn in the last 4 weeks that wasn't generated by a model? If the answer is nothing, the process has already begun.

FACE or RIP. The chess board is over. Now it's squash. And the match has already started.

Shall we begin?

posts.title: Using AI in Secret Isn't Paranoia. It's Logic.
posts.html:
40% of employees in professional services believe the AI they're adopting ends up eroding their own job security.

Read that again.

These aren't retail workers, assembly line operators, or admin staff. These are professionals. Consultants. People with your profile. And almost half suspect that the tool they're being asked to use is the same one that will eventually replace them.

In that context, hiding how much you use AI isn't paranoia. It's logic.

The Phenomenon Nobody Wants to Name

Shadow AI.

The term is used in IT to describe unauthorized use of AI tools within an organization. Employees feeding sensitive data into ChatGPT, consultants building deliverables with Claude without disclosing it, entire teams depending on Gemini to deliver but the report shows "consultant's work."

But there's another layer of Shadow AI that doesn't appear in IT governance reports. It's psychological. And it doesn't apply equally to all profiles.

Developers are at the opposite extreme. They post on LinkedIn about how much code they generated with Claude Code this week. They share their prompts on Twitter. They record videos using Cursor live. Their culture rewards showing the tool. Those who don't use it get left behind.

But go one floor down in the enterprise building and the culture inverts.

In SAP, in Big 4 consulting, in Compliance, legal, and corporate finance, the norm is the opposite. Nobody posts on LinkedIn that they resolved the reconciliation issue with Claude. Nobody says in the Daily that the requirements document was built with Gemini. Nobody mentions in the performance review interview that the functional blueprint was drafted with AI.

It gets done. But it doesn't get said.

The SAP Case: Two Stories That Probably Sound Familiar

Case 1. Senior SAP consultant. Asked for an impact analysis for an S/4HANA migration. Has the draft in 30 minutes with Claude, validated against 12 years of personal knowledge. Spends the next 3 hours reformatting, changing phrases, adding manual comments to make it look like it took all day. Delivers. Nobody asks how it was done. He doesn't say how it was done.

Case 2. Tech Lead on an implementation project. Discovers the ABAP a Junior built was generated entirely with SAP Joule for Developers. The Junior didn't understand the logic but the code "works and runs fine." The Tech Lead has three options: escalate and expose the Junior, take the time to do pair programming, or silently correct and let it pass. Chooses the third. Because if he escalates, it also gets exposed that he himself uses AI for 40% of his own work. And since the Manager is pressuring him with more parallel projects, he doesn't have time for the Junior.

Both are in Shadow AI. For different reasons. One out of fear of being seen as replaceable. The other out of silent complicity with a practice that's already massive but nobody wants to formalize.

The 81% vs 53% Paradox

A recent article in Harvard Business Review published data that explains why Shadow AI exists and won't disappear among the profiles that matter.

81% of senior leaders surveyed believe their organization is completely focused on Augmentation — using AI to enhance people.

Only 53% of individual contributors believe the same.

That 28-point gap is the fertile ground for Shadow AI. Your boss is convinced they're doing Augmentation. You read the newsletter where Jack Dorsey laid off 4,000 people from Block saying that intelligence tools changed what it means to build a company. And you do the math.

So the consultant uses Claude in secret, delivers better and faster, and nobody finds out. Because if they do, the Board's logical conclusion is:

"If with AI you can do twice as much, then we need half as many consultants, or we charge half as much for the same scope."

That reasoning is what kills trust throughout the chain. And it's already starting to do damage.

The Other Fire: Deliberate Degradation

And while the professional hides how much they use AI to avoid being seen as replaceable, something happens in parallel that deepens the trap.

The models are declining in quality.

We talked about it last week. Thousands of users documenting that outputs are worse, that they have to wait until a certain hour for the system not to be saturated, that the $20 subscription is no longer enough and you have to move to the $200 tier.

The acceleration was so brutal that it created dependency before anyone could question it. And now the supply seems to be adjusting — with erratic behavior between sessions, excessive token consumption, and other things you can read about in specialized forums.

It reminds me, as uncomfortable as the comparison is, of how dealers operate. First they give away the product. You develop dependency. You can no longer function without the tool that used to give you magical results. Then — only then — they adjust the price and the quantity.

Between one model and the next, only months pass. By the time you finish learning to work well with one, the next one arrives with different limitations, different behaviors, different prices.

The professional is trapped between two fires: if they show how much they use the tool, they get replaced. If they depend on the tool, their supply gets adjusted. Two traps feeding each other.

Augmentation vs Automation: The Choice That Determines Everything

There are two paths. Augmentation — using AI to enhance people. Automation — using AI to replace them.

Dorsey chose Automation. Block laid off 4,000 people. The message to shareholders was surgical: intelligence tools changed the equation for building a company.

Kaufman, the CEO of Fiverr, chose to tell his employees that AI is coming for everyone's work, including his own. But he didn't lay anyone off. He bet that each employee could develop capabilities AI doesn't have: non-linear judgment, taste decisions, strategic thinking.

Two CEOs, two opposite readings of the same moment.

The research cited by HBR shows something that should be obvious but is ignored: the Automation path yields quick gains but generates a predictable cascade. Resistance, loss of wellbeing, talent flight, employer brand erosion, collapse of leadership ranks. In six phases, what started as savings ends as organizational debt.

The Augmentation path has a deeper J-curve at the beginning. Investing in training people, redesigning processes, building human-AI coordination is expensive and slow. But what emerges on the other side isn't efficiency. It's a new productive frontier.

The problem is that financial markets reward Automation today. And the senior consultants reading this live in companies where the decision was already made, even if nobody announced it.

Antifragility: The Only Way Out

Nassim Taleb introduced a concept that gets misused constantly. Antifragility is not resistance.

The fragile breaks under shock. The resilient withstands the shock without breaking. The antifragile gets stronger from the shock.

Shadow AI is fragile. It depends on nobody finding out. The day the company audits actual AI usage, the consultant who was hiding is exposed with no argument.

Dependence on the model of the moment is also fragile. The day usage policy changes, the price goes up, or quality goes down, your workflow collapses.

Antifragility is something else. It's building a personal system that benefits from shocks.

What does that look like, concretely?

First, accumulated judgment that no model can replicate. The years of understanding why the data model is designed this way, why you chose X architecture, what happens when you touch a certain configuration. That doesn't get hidden. It gets shown. Because it's what amplifies the AI — not what AI replaces.

Second, personal systems that transcend the tool. If your workflow is tied to a specific model, you're fragile. If your workflow is "define the problem, document, validate with N available models, apply own judgment, iterate" — you're antifragile. When the model changes, the results change. You don't.

Third, documentation of your own process. The person who documents how they think, how they decide, how they combine tools builds an asset no employer can erase and no model can replicate. That documentation is your proof of work in a world where output is falsifiable.

Fourth, and this is counterintuitive: show how you use AI, don't hide it. But show it in a frame that makes clear you're the one who decides, orchestrates, and validates. Shadow AI makes you suspicious. Proof of work with AI makes you a reference.

That's exactly what developers have been doing for two years. And that's why they wrote the public narrative about Tech productivity — not the senior consultants who deliver three times as much but still bill man-hours as if nothing had changed.

The antifragile doesn't hide that they use AI. They use it in public, document the process, and let market shocks validate their system.

Your Diagnosis This Week

Three questions.

  1. Are you hiding how much you use AI in your work? If the answer is yes, the problem isn't AI. It's that you don't trust your judgment is visible without it. That's the gap you need to close.
  1. Does your workflow depend on a specific model? If tomorrow that model doubles its price, drops in quality, or disappears — what happens to your ability to deliver? If the answer is "I sink," you're fragile.
  1. Can you explain to someone how you decide when to use AI and when not to? If it takes you more than 30 seconds to answer, you don't have a system. You have a habit. Habits break when context changes.

Last week we talked about the external gap: who has access to which models. This week, the internal gap: who hides how they work and why.

Next week we build the system that makes you antifragile in both.

FACE or RIP. The board already changed. The question is whether you're playing defense or building the muscle that gets stronger with every shock.

Shall we begin?

Sources: De Neve, Hancock, and Niederhoffer. "Why Companies That Choose AI Augmentation Over Automation May Win in the Long Run." Harvard Business Review, April 2026. · Mo Gawdat, Scary Smart. · Nassim Nicholas Taleb, Antifragile, 2012. · Claude Mythos Preview System Card, Anthropic, April 2026. · Erik Brynjolfsson et al., research on the "Productivity J-Curve." · Indeed Workforce Insights Report 2025.

posts.title: SAP Just Created Two ERP Castes. And Most People Missed It.
posts.html:
DISCLAIMER: This article talks about SAP because that's the ecosystem I've worked in for many years. But if you work with Salesforce, Oracle, Workday, ServiceNow, or any dominant enterprise platform, what follows applies directly to you. All major vendors are making similar decisions right now. Change the name, the logic doesn't change.

There's a line in the new SAP API Policy v.4.2026 that went almost unnoticed.

It's not in the headlines. No Gartner analyst mentioned it this week. But if you've spent more than 10 years in the SAP ecosystem, when you read it, you feel that something changed permanently.

The line reads, verbatim:

"SAP prohibits the use of APIs for interaction or integration with autonomous or generative AI systems that plan, select, or execute sequences of API calls."

In plain English: SAP just legally blocked AI agents on its core.

Precisely when everyone is talking about agentic AI. Precisely when Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google are building the infrastructure for agents to execute complete business processes autonomously. Precisely at that moment, SAP closes the door they would need to walk through.

This is not a technical detail. It's a strategic move. And understanding who benefits and who gets hurt is exactly the work this article is here to do.

The Week Nobody Read Correctly

In the same week SAP published that API Policy, Christian Klein, SAP's CEO, warned internally that the transition to AI will be as difficult as the transition to cloud.

That's not a minor statement. The cloud transition took SAP nearly a decade (and it's still ongoing), a brutal number of resistant customers, investor pressure, internal restructurings, and the creation of S/4HANA as a replacement product for ECC. It was painful, slow, and costly for everyone involved.

Klein is saying what's coming is equally large — or worse.

And simultaneously, his legal team published a document that defines with surgical precision where artificial intelligence can and cannot enter their platform.

Two coordinated moves. One clear message: SAP is going to control how AI enters its ecosystem. And whoever doesn't read it that way will end up on the wrong side of this transition.

The Triangle SAP Already Built

To understand why the API Policy has the timing it has, you need to see the full board.

In September 2025, SAP announced the partnership with OpenAI to launch "OpenAI for Germany" — a digital sovereignty project for the German public sector. The stack is clear: SAP as the enterprise layer, OpenAI as the model, Microsoft Azure as cloud infrastructure.

It's the perfect triangle for the hyper-regulated European market. Three companies that need each other, with service level agreements, compliance, data sovereignty, and coordinated roadmaps.

SAP chose its AI partner. Its name is OpenAI. And the infrastructure is Microsoft.

In last week's earnings call, Klein was asked directly whether clients are building agents outside the SAP ecosystem using SAP data, and whether SAP has enough control over that access.

His answer was unambiguous. Customer data belongs to the customer — SAP won't charge for accessing it. But SAP's IP — the semantic models, ontologies, process knowledge — that they will protect and distribute exclusively through their platform.

It's not "no APIs for AI." It's "use our APIs — the ones we publish, govern, monitor, and control."

There's an entire ecosystem of partners, vendors, and integrators who today have solutions built on interfaces that tomorrow may fall outside the official perimeter. For them, this is not a security policy. It's a direct threat to their business model. The questions aren't answered. They're just beginning.

Anthropic is not in that triangle. And that matters more than it seems.

Because while SAP builds its official ecosystem with OpenAI, there are developers, partners, and clients using Claude Code to build solutions on SAP. Consuming APIs. Automating processes. Creating extensions that SAP didn't design, didn't publish, and doesn't control. The best SAP MCPs I've seen to date have Claude making 99% of the commits.

The API Policy doesn't mention Anthropic by name. It doesn't have to. The language is broad enough to cover any AI agent that executes sequences of calls on SAP core. Any of them.

Is it a coincidence that the document appears at exactly the moment when AI agents are becoming technically capable of orchestrating complete SAP processes? I don't believe in those coincidences.

The Problem Nobody Talks About: Half the SAP World Is Still on ECC

Here's where the biggest paradox of this entire move appears.

SAP has spent years pressuring everyone to migrate to S/4HANA. The ECC support deadline has moved several times, but the direction is clear: at some point support ends and whoever hasn't migrated is exposed. No security patches. No updates. No official ecosystem.

SAP's argument for migration was always the same: S/4HANA is more secure, more modern, more prepared for the future.

But more than half of SAP customers haven't started the migration. Not for lack of desire. Because of cost, complexity, operational risk, and in many cases because the ECC they have works perfectly for what they need. The business doesn't stop because SAP launched a new product.

And now a variable appears that SAP didn't calculate in its business model a few years back.

The '67 Shelby Cobra With Nitro and Armor

Imagine a 1967 Mustang Shelby Cobra. Original engine, classic body, the roar no modern car can replicate. Years of road testing. Accumulated judgment in every bolt.

Now imagine someone installs millimeter-precision electronic fuel injection, real-time telemetry connected to the cloud, an AI collision-avoidance system processing the environment at 200 frames per second, active cybersecurity shielding that detects and neutralizes vulnerabilities before they can be exploited, and an AI co-pilot that learns how you drive and optimizes performance based on your style.

Is it an old car? No. It's a car with decades of accumulated judgment plus today's technological muscle. It's better than many new cars in what matters: it knows its own guts, has a history of real behavior, and now has the intelligence to anticipate what's coming. And maybe with a not-so-"Clean Core" mindset as its mantra.

That's exactly what's happening with SAP ECC in 2026 — or will happen, if the math holds.

A client with a well-implemented ECC has something no new S/4 installation has: years of structured data from real operations, mature processes, proven integrations, and a team that understands why each configuration exists. That can't be migrated. It's built over years.

The question nobody was asking until now is: what if instead of migrating that ECC to S/4HANA, we plug in an enterprise-grade AI model that gives it next-generation security, agentic capabilities, intelligent consumption of existing data, and the ability to evolve its capabilities independently of SAP's roadmap?

The answer SAP doesn't want you to consider: maybe you don't need to migrate. It sounds crazy, but I know many people are thinking it and even talking about it. It's still some time away from being 100% feasible — but for how long?

The Two ERP Castes

What's emerging is not a technical decision. It's a strategic bifurcation that will define the SAP ecosystem for the next decade.

Caste 1: Those who migrated to S/4HANA and married the official ecosystem.

They have Joule as their native co-pilot. They have access to BTP with published and supported APIs. They have the SAP/OpenAI/Microsoft triangle as their AI stack. They have official support, clear roadmap, guaranteed compliance. They might even know how the AI units on their next month's invoice are calculated.

And they have a restriction they signed in the contract: the intelligence they can use on their core is the intelligence SAP decides they can use.

Caste 2: The rebels who stayed on ECC.

They have a platform SAP considers legacy. That at some point will lose official support. That doesn't have S/4's innovation roadmap.

But they also have something the first caste doesn't: the freedom to choose what intelligence they plug into their system.

If an external AI model — enterprise-grade or better — can provide active security that detects and neutralizes vulnerabilities in real time, can consume and structure ECC data with the same depth as any native tool, can orchestrate complex business processes on that ECC without needing SAP's published APIs because it works at a different layer, and can evolve its capabilities autonomously, independent of SAP's product cycle and adapting to each thing the user asks —

Some will say it'll be a Frankenstein pumped with AI steroids. But if in the end that's what the user wants and it carries their business model, there's nothing more to discuss for many.

Then SAP's main argument for migration — security and technological modernity — weakens significantly.

It doesn't disappear. There are cases where S/4HANA is clearly superior and migration makes sense. But it stops being the only possible answer.

And that's what SAP sees coming. That's why the API Policy has the language it has.

The Tension Klein Can't Easily Resolve

Christian Klein is in a position that looks a lot like the record industry leaders' in 2001, when the iPod appeared.

You can try to close the legal fence around your platform so external intelligence can't enter without your permission. That's what the API Policy does. It has logic from the perspective of control, compliance, and business model.

Or you can open up strategically and become the platform on which the ecosystem's intelligence is built. Where any agent, from any vendor, wants to be certified to run on SAP because that's where the data that matters lives.

The problem is those two options are almost contradictory. And SAP, right now, appears to be choosing the first.

The history of the cloud transition shows what happens when vendors try to control too tightly a technology transition that clients didn't choose. The resistance was massive. The timelines moved several times. And the migration still isn't complete.

If history repeats, SAP's attempt to control how AI enters its ecosystem may generate exactly the opposite effect: accelerating the search for alternatives by clients who don't want to depend on a single stack.

What This Means for You

Three profiles and three different realities.

If you're on an active S/4HANA migration project: The API Policy doesn't change anything for you in the short term. You stay on the official path. But pay close attention to how the agent ecosystem evolves on BTP. What SAP allows or doesn't allow in that layer will define how much real value you can extract from the stack.

If you're managing an ECC nobody wants to migrate: This is your window. Not to resist migration indefinitely, but to reframe the conversation with your organization. The question is no longer just "how much does migration cost" but "what alternatives exist to modernize the intelligence layer without touching the core." That conversation is worth more every day.

If you're a partner or independent consultant: The most interesting niche in the next 24 months is not standard S/4HANA implementation. It's AI architecture on legacy systems. Someone has to design how an external model consumes, processes, and orchestrates on an ECC. That someone needs to actually understand SAP — not just know how to use Joule.

If you replace "SAP" with the name of the vendor you depend on, the question is the same. And so is the urgency.

Your Diagnosis This Week

The 3 questions.

  1. Do you know exactly which APIs you're using in your integrations, and which of them are published versus non-published according to the new API Policy? If the answer is no, you have a compliance risk your organization probably doesn't know it has.
  1. Does your organization have a defined position on the AI stack it will use on SAP? Or are they waiting for SAP to tell them what to use? If they're waiting, they already made a decision without knowing it.
  1. Can you articulate in a meeting with your CIO why the ECC vs S/4 debate changed with the emergence of enterprise-grade AI models? If you can't, someone else will have that conversation — probably with less judgment than you.

The last three weeks built a trilogy without planning it.

Week 1: The external gap. Who has access to the most powerful models and who doesn't. Mythos as a symbol of a two-speed world.

Week 2: The internal gap. Who hides how they work with AI and why that's a trap. Shadow AI as poorly calibrated survival logic.

Week 3 (this edition): The ecosystem gap. SAP building the fence precisely when external intelligence has for the first time the technical capability to bypass it.

The three gaps feed each other. And all three have the same blind spot in common: the professional who doesn't take an active position gets trapped in the middle.

The '67 Mustang isn't a nostalgic metaphor. It's a strategic question. How much is accumulated judgment worth when intelligence can be plugged in from the outside?

The answer to that question is worth more than any S/4HANA certification you could earn this year.

Pablo / Above Average

Sources: SAP API Policy v.4.2026. SAP x OpenAI "OpenAI for Germany" announcement, September 2025. Bloomberg, Christian Klein statements on the AI transition, April 2026. Claude Mythos Preview System Card, Anthropic, April 2026.
