posts.title: AI Lied to My Face. Microsoft Just Explained Why.
posts.html:
It was a Tuesday morning. I was reviewing a SAP project in production. I asked Joule, the AI copilot integrated into SAP, to explain what a specific ABAP class did. It returned a perfect explanation. Detailed. Structured. Logical. And completely false. The class didn't exist. Joule invented it. And presented it to me as if it were real.

If I had believed it at first glance, that explanation would have gone into a technical document. From there to an architecture decision. From there to a migration. And three weeks later, the error would have exploded in some production environment where nobody would have understood what happened.

But something made me doubt. It wasn't technical intuition. It was something stranger. The explanation Joule gave me was too clean. Too well-structured. In 20 years in production, I learned that real code is never clean. It always has patches, odd historical decisions, comments a consultant left 8 years ago, logic that seems arbitrary but responds to something that happened in a 2015 migration.

What Joule showed me looked like a textbook. And real code is not a textbook. So I did something a junior probably wouldn't have done. I passed the full context to Gemini and asked it to explain the same class.

Gemini told me, verbatim, that the class didn't exist in the namespace I mentioned. That there was probably a naming confusion. And it suggested three similar classes that did exist.

One AI had lied to me. Another corrected it. The one who made the final decision was me. Not any AI. Most importantly: that type of error is happening right now, in thousands of companies, every day. And most professionals are not catching it.

A couple of weeks ago, Microsoft Research published a study that explains exactly what happened to me that morning. And what's happening in thousands of offices as you read this.

The Narrative You Hear Every Day

The public narrative about AI at work today is a single one.

Block laid off four thousand people last year citing AI productivity. Microsoft, Meta, Google — they're all doing the same. And they're not stopping.

The conclusion your boss, your CEO, Wall Street analysts, and LinkedIn influencers are drawing is the same: AI replaces the knowledge worker. Period. Those who don't adapt get left behind.

That narrative is real. Companies keep laying people off like there's no tomorrow. I won't lie to you about that.

But there's another part of the story that almost nobody is sharing. And it changes the entire conversation.

The Paper That Changes the Debate

Microsoft Research just published the most comprehensive study that exists on what happens when you delegate professional work to AI over a sustained period.

It's called DELEGATE-52. Released in April 2026.

Microsoft took 52 professional domains. Not just programming. Accounting, recipes, musical scores, molecular structures, calendars, architectural blueprints — everything a professional edits day to day.

Then they took 19 artificial intelligence models. The world's best and worst. And simulated what happens when a user delegates real tasks.

Each task is reversible. You ask the AI to change something, then ask it to return to the original state. If the AI is good, the final document must be identical to the initial one.

After 20 back-and-forths, no model in the world manages to keep the document intact.

The best models — the frontier ones, the ones under lock and key, the ones your company licenses — corrupt 25% of the document. The worse models corrupt 50%.

And here's the data point you need to tattoo on your arm.

In 80% of existing professions, after 20 interactions, AI introduces errors severe enough that the work becomes corrupted.

Do you know which is the only domain where most models perform well?

Python. Pure programming.

AI is good at programming — we know that. But it's mediocre at almost everything else, or at least that's what the results say.

I call this the false benchmark trap. Companies measure AI capability in programming, see it works well, and extrapolate. They assume if it works in code, it works in accounting, consulting, business analysis, anywhere. Microsoft just proved that extrapolation is false.

AI is a specialist that believes it's a generalist. And companies are delegating generalist work to it.

The Invisible Error

But the most important part of the paper isn't the percentages. It's how AI makes mistakes. When the researchers analyzed the error patterns, they found something almost nobody is processing.

AI doesn't fail gradually. It's not that each interaction introduces a small error that accumulates over many iterations. AI maintains near-perfection for several turns. And then, without warning, in a single interaction, it destroys 10, 20, 30 points of the document in one shot.

80% of the total damage comes from single catastrophic failures. I call this the invisible error.

It's invisible because you won't detect it by reviewing interaction by interaction. The first looks perfect. The second looks perfect. The third looks perfect. And then, at some random moment, the AI deletes an entire section, invents data that wasn't there, mixes information it shouldn't mix.

And the document goes to production with that error inside. Think about what this means for your work. If you're the Tech Lead, the consultant, the analyst who approves the output the AI generated, you're the last filter. But if you review the first three versions and they were all fine, you'll trust it. And the fourth one has the catastrophic error.

That's the trap. The first successes generate the trust that later leads you to release control. And there's something even worse. Weaker models tend to delete content. Better models — the frontier ones — tend to corrupt content.

Deleted content is noticeable. It's missing. Corrupted content stays there, looks correct, but is wrong. And that error passes to the next person who reads the document. And the next. And the next. Until someone with judgment catches it. Or until the error explodes in production.

That's exactly what almost happened to me that morning with Joule. The explanation was plausible, structured, professional. If I'd put it in a document, the error would have been buried for weeks. Until someone tried to compile the real code and realized the referenced class didn't exist.

The Agentic Promise That Doesn't Deliver

There's one more data point from the paper you need to know. Because it changes the decision your company is probably about to make.

For months the industry has been selling the concept of autonomous AI agents. The promise is simple: pure AI is good but limited. If you give it tools, allow it to execute code, read and write files, do multiple steps — then it becomes much more capable.

That's the central promise of Claude Code. Of OpenAI Codex. Of Joule for Developers. Of the entire new generation of enterprise products.

Microsoft tested exactly that. They took the same models, gave them tool access, put them in agentic mode. The models with tools perform worse than without tools. Six percent additional degradation. The central promise of enterprise agentic products, experimentally tested, is false. It's not that the tools don't help. It's that they make the result worse.

Why? Because tools consume context. They generate overhead. Models make wrong decisions about when to use which tool. They invent files that don't exist, execute code they shouldn't, read information that confuses the output.

Meanwhile, enterprise vendors are closing their ecosystems so that only their official agents can execute on your data. SAP last week updated its API Policy blocking external agents. Salesforce, Oracle — everyone is on the same path.

Connect the dots. Vendors are blocking external agents on their platforms. The official agents they themselves sell, according to Microsoft Research, don't work as well as promised. And clients are massively migrating to that stack because they were told it's the future.

I call this the blind migration. We're building massive technological dependency on tools that the research itself shows aren't ready for production in most domains.

The Veteran Bonus

If models corrupt 25% of work, if agents make the result worse, if the first interactions generate a false sense of confidence that later leads you to release control — then someone needs to be there to catch all of that.

That someone is you. If you have accumulated judgment. I call this the veteran bonus.

It's the market phase where the professional with 15 or 20 years of real experience — who understands how their domain behaves under load, who knows what things can break, who has intuition about which output doesn't make sense — is worth more than ever.

Not because they're better than AI at everything. They're better than AI at some things and worse at others.

They're worth more because they're the only one who can detect when AI fails. And AI fails more than people believe.

The tech consultant who has spent years understanding how an integration behaves under real load can look at an output Joule generated and say "this doesn't add up." The senior financial analyst can look at a report assembled by AI and say "this number doesn't make sense." The doctor with 20 years of experience can look at an AI-assisted diagnosis and say "something doesn't fit here."

That ability to detect the invisible error is what companies are laying off when they do mass cuts.

Firing the person who detects the error to trust the tool that produces the error. It's mathematically absurd. But it's exactly what's happening.

And the consequence will be felt in 12 to 18 months, when compounded errors start appearing in production and nobody in the organization has the judgment to understand where they came from. That's where your opportunity appears.

The 3 Rules I Apply Every Day

After that morning with Joule, I adjusted three things in how I work. I'm sharing them because they're the only ones that matter to me today.

Rule 1: Never delegate more than you can review.

If you're going to use AI for a professional task, mentally divide the task into blocks you can review. If the task is generating 10 reports, don't ask for all 10 at once. Ask for one, review it, ask for the second, review it. And so on.

The reason is structural. The Microsoft paper proves the failure appears in a specific interaction. If you're not present at that interaction, the error passes.

That morning with Joule, what saved me was exactly that. I asked for a single explanation. When something sounded off, I stopped. If I had asked for five explanations of five different classes in a single prompt, I probably wouldn't have caught the error.

Human review is not optional. It's the only real filter that exists today.

Rule 2: Document your judgment, not just your outputs.

Every time you catch an AI error, document it. Note what you saw, how you realized it, what would have happened if you hadn't caught it. That's irrefutable proof of work.

Your organization will start to see, in 6 to 12 months, that it needs people with judgment. Those who documented their catches will be the ones who stay. Those who only delivered outputs without context will be interchangeable with the AI that generates them.

After that morning, I opened a private document where I note every time an AI lies to me. I have almost 20 entries in a few months. That's my proof that judgment still matters.

Rule 3: Build model independence.

Never use a single AI model. Use at least two in rotation. When one fails, the other shows you it failed. When one changes its usage policy, the other saves you.

If that morning I had only had Joule, I wouldn't have caught anything. What saved me was having Gemini alongside to cross-check. That redundancy is the difference between detecting an invisible error and letting it pass.

Dependence on a single provider is the most expensive technical mistake you can make in your professional career in the coming years.

Your Diagnosis This Week

That morning when Joule lied to me, it could have been a bad day. It could have been the first of many times an invisible error passed into production and I appeared, months later, trying to explain where it came from.

But it wasn't. And it wasn't luck. It was accumulated judgment. It was years of looking at real code and knowing that real code is never clean. It was having a second AI alongside to cross-check. It was documenting the incident so as not to forget it.

The professional of the immediate future is not the one who executes fastest with AI. It's the one who detects when AI fails. And that capability can't be bought. It's built with years of doing the work by hand before having shortcuts.

If you have those years behind you, this is the best moment of your career. Even though the news says otherwise.

Three questions for this week.

When was the last time you caught an AI error? If the answer is "I don't remember" or "never," you're probably not reviewing enough. And the errors are happening.

Does your organization have a policy for how AI output is reviewed before it goes to production? If it doesn't, you have an opportunity to propose one. And to be the one who proposed it.

Can you explain to someone how you decide when to trust an AI and when not to? If it takes you more than 30 seconds to answer, your trust is by default. And default trust is the first step toward the invisible error.

Over the last few weeks I talked about three gaps AI is opening in professional work. The external gap — who has access to premium models. The internal gap — who hides how they work with AI. The ecosystem gap — vendors closing access just as agents would start working.

This week I close the trilogy with the fourth gap that needs to be named. The judgment gap. The one that separates those who detect the invisible error from those who let it pass.

Your role is not the one who executes. It's the one who detects when execution fails.

Sources:

Laban, Schnabel, Neville. "LLMs Corrupt Your Documents When You Delegate." Microsoft Research, April 2026. arXiv:2604.15597.

SAP API Policy v.4.2026.

Bloomberg, Christian Klein statements on the AI transition, April 2026.

Claude Mythos Preview System Card, Anthropic, April 2026.

posts.title: While Everyone Talks Layoffs, Accenture Talks Promotions
posts.html:
Today, SAP Sapphire kicks off in Orlando.

Christian Klein is about to take the stage and announce SAP AI Foundation. A unified layer that connects SAP's own models with external models: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, all of them. On SAP data. Governed by SAP.

The official narrative is that SAP is opening its ecosystem. The alternative narrative — the one some consultants are already publishing — is that SAP is installing new bars on the cage.

Both are true at the same time. But that's not the point. The point is what you're going to do when Klein's keynote ends. Because while the rest of the industry debates whether SAP is opening or closing, you have a different job. And that job needs a name.

The Operator

There's a scene in The Matrix that almost nobody mentions, but that defines the entire system. When Neo, Trinity, and the others enter the simulation, there's someone sitting in the hovercraft, in front of several monitors with green code cascading down. His name is Tank. And later, Link.

Tank doesn't enter the Matrix. He doesn't fight. He doesn't run. He doesn't dodge bullets.

Tank loads programs into Neo's mind. When Neo needs to fly a helicopter, Tank loads the piloting program. When Trinity needs karate, Tank loads the karate program. When someone needs to recognize a pattern in code scrolling at 200 km/h, Tank activates it from the outside.

Tank is not the hero. He's the Operator.

He's the one with full system visibility, the one who decides what capability goes in when, the one who orchestrates those in the field. Without the Operator, the subversive agents are blind. With the Operator, they're lethal. That's the most accurate metaphor I know for what it means to work in Technology in 2026.

The Difference from MacGyver

If you're over 35 and watched any TV, you probably remember MacGyver. That 80s show where the protagonist solved any problem with what he had in his pocket. A Swiss Army knife, duct tape, a paperclip, a lighter. He never used a weapon. He always used judgment.

The Operator's spirit is the same as MacGyver's. The problem solver. The one who looks at the room and says "with what I have here, I can build something."

But the Operator's practice is Tank's. Because MacGyver worked alone. With his two hands. In 2026, you don't just have two hands. You also have access to Claude, Gemini, Joule, multiple MCPs, dozens of specialized AI agents.

The modern senior consultant is no longer just a MacGyver. They're Tank with MacGyver's spirit. The one who knows which agent to load, when to load it, what data to feed it, which output to review, and when to discard everything and start over.

And this isn't theory. It's exactly what SAP is about to announce in Orlando.

What's Coming at Sapphire

The centerpiece of the announcement is called SAP AI Foundation.

It's a unified infrastructure that connects SAP's own AI models (Joule and derivatives) with external models: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Cohere. All of them. Operating on SAP data, in a layer that SAP governs.

Klein said it explicitly in his last earnings call: "infuse 50 years of domain knowledge into AI agents and govern the agentic layer for our clients."

Translated: SAP understood it can't compete against frontier models. So it decided to become the gateway where those models plug into SAP data. You don't call Anthropic directly. You call SAP's layer, which calls Anthropic, which returns to SAP's layer, which returns to you.

SAP isn't opening. SAP is becoming the toll booth.

This is the opposite of what Tobias Hofmann wrote last week on his blog. Hofmann argues that SAP doesn't innovate out of love for the customer — it innovates to stay relevant. To have a seat at the table where strategic decisions are made at Fortune 500 companies. And the SAP API Policy, that document we analyzed two weeks ago, isn't protection. It's controlled exclusion.

There are other SAP voices that went viral last week defending the API Policy. The argument — which I respect — is that SAP built the ecosystem over decades and has the right to defend it from predators who come to copy it, wrap it with AI, and sell it back. The phrase was: "I support AI. But I do NOT support predators."

Both positions can be true. SAP has the right to defend its ecosystem. And at the same time, SAP's decisions respond to SAP's interests, not always to its own customers'.

The point here isn't choosing sides. It's understanding the full play so you can make your own decision.

The Veteran Bonus, Revisited

Last week I talked about the Microsoft Research paper showing that AI corrupts 25% of work after 20 interactions. I talked about the veteran bonus — that market phase where the professional with accumulated judgment is worth more than ever because they're the only one who can detect the invisible error.

This week, that argument deepens.

The veteran bonus isn't just detecting errors. It's deciding the complete architecture. Which model to use for which task, which MCPs to connect, which agents to load, when to trust the output, when to discard it.

The veteran isn't the one who executes fastest. They're the Operator who decides what gets executed.

And SAP, with its AI Foundation, is building the perfect stage for Operators with judgment to shine and executors without judgment to be exposed.

When all your colleagues have access to Joule, Claude, and Gemini through the SAP layer, the difference won't be in who uses which tool. Everyone will have the same ones. The difference will be in who knows how to combine them with judgment.

What Accenture Just Admitted

As I write this, Accenture CEO Julie Sweet was giving an interview in Las Vegas where she acknowledged something almost nobody wants to say out loud. Her exact words: companies are investing massively in AI but struggling to translate that investment into measurable transformation.

The solution Accenture applied — what Sweet called "a playbook" — was tying internal promotions to each employee's AI fluency level. Not tool usage. Fluency.

That word matters. Fluency isn't knowing prompts. Fluency is knowing when to use what, with what judgment, on what data, for what purpose. Fluency is being an Operator.

Accenture, the world's largest consulting firm, just publicly acknowledged that the problem isn't AI. It's the judgment to use it. And it's reorganizing its entire operating model around that idea.

While the public narrative keeps talking about mass AI layoffs, Accenture is talking about promotions tied to AI fluency. Both things are happening in parallel, but they mean the opposite. One assumes the worker is replaceable. The other recognizes that accumulated judgment is promotable.

If Accenture is doing it, your company will be doing it in 6 to 18 months. And if you're not positioned as an Operator by then, you'll be the executor they evaluate — not the operator they promote.

What's Happening in the Trenches, Meanwhile

Microsoft publishes papers on AI's real capabilities. Accenture restructures its operating model. SAP announces AI Foundation. All of this happens on big stages, with lights and press coverage.

Meanwhile, in the professional forums where people talk without filters, the conversation is different. This week I read an architect on an enterprise project describe their week: they had to review two architecture documents and two test plans. All written with AI, with ten bullet points per section. And by Friday their eyes hurt from reading and reviewing every corner for details the AI missed. That the other developers also missed.

What bothered them most, they said, is that the work falls on the ones who have to review and approve these things. Everyone else gets comfortable with a few prompts, downloads pages and pages of generated text, and hands it to someone else to review. Forcing the reviewer to think twice as hard.

The post went viral because the complaint resonated.

On the other hand, veteran ERP analyst Jon Reed from Diginomica put it more bluntly this week. Reed expressed his frustration with the current narrative about AI agents replacing enterprise systems. And left a line that captures the entire problem in seven words:

Your probabilistic agent cannot complete deterministic workflows.

That line is the best technical description that exists today of AI's real limit in professional enterprise work.

A deterministic workflow is one where the result must be exact. Moving money between accounts. Closing an accounting period. Running payroll. Executing an SAP migration. Things where "almost correct" is the same as incorrect.

A probabilistic agent is what AI is. A machine that generates responses based on probabilities. Most of the time it's right. Sometimes it's not. And when it's not, it doesn't tell you.

Crossing those two categories without human judgment is exactly what the Microsoft Research paper showed fails. It's exactly what the architect I mentioned is suffering through when reviewing unfiltered generated documents. And it's exactly what Accenture is trying to avoid by tying promotions to fluency.

Two types of professionals are emerging right now. Those who use AI to generate volume fast without filtering the output. And those with the judgment to know where the probabilistic agent cannot complete the deterministic workflow.

The first group feels productive. The second group feels exploited. And the second group is who Accenture is promoting, who Reed is defending, and who your organization will need when the first group causes the first serious production incident.

If you're in the second group, you're not suffering the problem. You're the Operator.

The 3 Rules of the Operator

Rule 1: Know your models. Don't just use one.

Each model has different strengths. Claude is better for long-form reasoning and programming. Gemini is better for fact-checking. OpenAI models are better for structured tasks. Joule is better for native SAP context.

If you only know one, you're hostage to its weaknesses. The Operator knows the catalog and chooses by task.

Rule 2: Document your decision chain, not just your output.

When you solve something by combining three models and two MCPs, what needs to be documented isn't the final result. It's the path. Why you chose Claude for the first step, why you switched to Gemini to verify, why you discarded Joule's output.

That documentation is your proof of work as an Operator. It's what no CEO can automate yet. And it's exactly the type of fluency that Accenture just declared as the basis for promotion.

Rule 3: Build platform independence.

SAP AI Foundation is going to make it very easy to stay inside the SAP ecosystem. Technological laziness will push you toward using only the official connectors, the certified agents, the endorsed tools.

Keep at least one external MCP server active, one model outside SAP's gateway, one tool you control. Independence is expensive today, but the day SAP changes its API Policy again (and they will), that independence will save you.

The Questions for This Week

As Sapphire unfolds in Orlando, you'll see an avalanche of announcements. Joule reimagined. SAP AI Foundation. Partnerships with Microsoft, OpenAI, Anthropic. Agents for finance, supply chain, HR.

The question isn't what SAP announces. The question is what decisions you make as an Operator after hearing the announcements.

Will you stay inside the SAP ecosystem, depending on Klein's decisions?

Will you leave entirely, assuming you can operate without the SAP layer?

Or will you take the harder but more profitable position — the Operator who moves in and out at will, connecting the best of inside with the best of outside, with accumulated judgment and documented proof of work?

There's no correct answer. But there's your answer. And that answer is what will define your career over the next 24 months.

Those who understand the fundamentals orchestrate. Those who don't are orchestrated.

This week in Orlando, SAP will show you the stage where the game will be played. You decide whether you enter as a spectator, a piece, or an Operator.

Sources:

Julie Sweet, CEO Accenture, interview with Fortune at the Great Place to Work For All Summit, Las Vegas, May 10, 2026.

Jon Reed, veteran ERP analyst at Diginomica, statements published on LinkedIn, May 2026.

Public discussion in professional developer forums on Reddit, May 2026.

Tobias Hofmann, "It's about control," itsfullofstars.de, May 2026.

Christian Klein, SAP Q1 2026 earnings call.

Constellation Research, "SAP Sapphire 2026 themes," April 2026.

Sapphire Innovation Guide, sap.com.

Previous newsletters referenced: Mythos (the access gap), Shadow AI (the internal gap), SAP API Policy (the two SAP castes), DELEGATE-52 (the judgment gap).

posts.title: SAP Promised the Autonomous Future. Reality Is Moving Slower.
posts.html:
Last week, Christian Klein took the stage in Orlando and promised the autonomous enterprise. 224 specialized agents. 51 Joule Assistants. Autonomous finance. Autonomous supply chain. Autonomous HR. Autonomous procurement. A complete vision where software runs the company and humans only step in for exceptions.

It sounds like the most ambitious announcement in SAP's recent history. While Klein was talking, SAP stock closed the week at 144 EUR. A drop of 28.67% year-to-date. That's not a coincidence. It's a signal.

This week I want to talk about the gap between the vision SAP sold in Orlando and the reality that field professionals are living. And inside that gap, there are three opportunities that can change your role — if you understand them in time.

The Vision They Sold

Before getting into the critique, it's worth acknowledging the scale of what was announced.

SAP Business AI Platform unifies BTP, Business Data Cloud, and AI Foundation into a single architecture. There was rebranding, but there are also genuinely new pieces. The Knowledge Graph maps over 7 million data fields. The new Company Memory layer captures "tribal knowledge" — policy documents, email approval chains — and converts them into what SAP calls Process Atoms. Joule Studio 2.0 promises to build agents in minutes instead of weeks. The SAP Autonomous Suite deploys agents across 224 business processes. The AI Agent Hub centralizes governance of both first-party and third-party agents.

The success stories they showcased are real:

  • Levi Strauss deployed over 1,000 agents and reduced manual wholesale order processing from 2-5 days to 20 minutes.
  • Aeropuertos Argentina built a Snow Agent that coordinates weather sensors and maintenance, with an MVP delivered in 12 weeks and a 16% cost reduction.
  • ExxonMobil is extracting the "trapped asset" of 150 years of legacy data toward a cloud-first posture.

If all of that works as announced, it changes enterprise work at its core.

But that's the key word. If.

The vision is exciting, but vision and reality are two different things. That's an operational observation, not an ideological one. The majority of the announced agents are in Early Adopter or Preview status. Meanwhile, the 2027 deadline for Solution Manager is still there. And the concrete scoping and execution work for the ALM transition doesn't change with an announcement.

I've participated in workshops with the creators of CALM, and today the tool is still far from what CHARM offers at the Deployment Management level. But there's already an agent to help you do Deployments (?).

This isn't hater skepticism. It's the voice of the people who have to implement what was announced.

The 3 Questions the Market Is Asking

After Sapphire, a lot of people with industry scars — not vendors — are asking some questions.

1. Who pays for all this intelligence?

This week Melissa Rosenthal published a data point that almost nobody is talking about. Every AI company serving your organization is currently losing money on you. The compute costs ten times what they're charging. We're all building on subsidized pricing.

Claude Pro costs $20 a month. The compute to serve a power user costs between $200 and $400. Microsoft was losing $80 per month on some Copilot users who were paying $10.

What does this mean? That providers are buying market share with subsidized prices. Your teams integrate AI into everything. AI becomes infrastructure. And then prices go up and you can't get out.

GitHub migrated Copilot to usage-based billing last month. OpenAI launched a $100/month tier. Anthropic has a $200 top plan. Microsoft raised 365 prices twice in four years, pointing directly at AI costs.

SAP is building on top of that same stack. And adds its own layer of AI Units whose real pricing nobody fully understands. If a company wants to implement the Autonomous Enterprise, they don't just pay for RISE. They pay AI Units. They pay BTP. They pay Joule per user. They pay specialized consulting.

The question no CFO is asking out loud yet: what happens when providers start charging the real price for their compute.

The 28.67% year-to-date drop in SAP stock suggests the market is already starting to do that math.

2. Who operates all this when the systems are fragile?

Anyone who knows even the basics of architecture knows that autonomy can't be built on fragile foundations.

You can't have 224 autonomous agents operating on poorly modeled data, poorly documented processes, and legacy code without Clean Core. That's not transformation. That's technical debt with new syntax, and it's going to blow up in the face of whoever manages that implementation. And no AI is going to save them from it.

SAP's own presentation implicitly acknowledges this. The new migration ToolChain promises a 50% reduction in effort, but requires Clean Core certification. The seven migration-specialized Joule Assistants exist precisely because without them the complexity is unmanageable.

And here's the uncomfortable data point. The majority of large companies that SAP serves are mid-RISE. Some systems in the cloud, others on-premise, integrations half-built, data mid-migration. Trying to push autonomous agents into that is not naivety. It's an invitation to operational disaster.

SAP's own announcement that on-premise clients will only have access to AI scenarios during the cloud transition confirms this. Autonomy is coupled to migration. And migration doesn't accelerate because of announcements.

3. Who detects when the agents fail?

Here I return to something I wrote two weeks ago. The Jon Reed line that captures the core technical problem of this entire architecture:

Your probabilistic agent cannot complete deterministic workflows.

SAP runs deterministic processes. Closing an accounting period. Running payroll. Moving money between accounts. Executing a migration. Things where "almost correct" is the same as "incorrect."

When you put 224 AI agents on top of those processes, you can't assume they'll fail visibly. The DELEGATE-52 paper from Microsoft Research showed that frontier models corrupt 25% of work after 20 interactions. And they do it without warning.

SAP is trying to address this with two interesting technical bets. The RPT-1.5 model is designed for explainability: users can trace the rows and columns that led to a prediction. And the NVIDIA OpenShell integration creates architectural barriers to prevent agents from accessing data they shouldn't see.

Those are serious bets. But explainable is not the same as correct. Auditable is not the same as reliable. And an agent operating within guardrails can still make wrong decisions within those guardrails.

The question then is not whether the agent can execute the task. The question is who verifies the output before it reaches production.

That question has only one answer. The Operator with accumulated judgment.

The Three Rules That Deepen Post-Sapphire

Two weeks ago I wrote about the three rules of the Operator. After Sapphire, those rules need an additional layer.

Rule 1: Know your models. Don't just use one.

Post-Sapphire, this gets more complex. SAP Business AI Platform integrates Claude from Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, and Cohere models — through SAP. Which means your access to those models goes through SAP's toll gate. Keep direct access to at least one model outside that ecosystem. The day SAP changes its AI Units pricing, that independence might save you.

Rule 2: Document your judgment. Not just your outputs.

There's a SAP play here worth understanding carefully. The Company Memory layer is designed to capture the organization's tribal knowledge and convert it into Process Atoms that agents can consume.

Read that twice. SAP literally wants to absorb the accumulated judgment of Operators and turn it into an algorithm.

That's not bad in itself. It's logical from the vendor's side. But as a professional, what will differentiate you when everyone on your team has access to the same 224 agents through the same SAP layer, is the documented record of well-made decisions that are not in Company Memory. Your decision portfolio is your new curriculum. And it must live outside the system that wants to absorb it.

Rule 3: Build platform independence.

Post-Sapphire, this becomes critical. SAP is gradually closing its API ecosystem to keep value inside its stack. That's legitimate SAP strategy. But as a professional, if you're 100% inside that ecosystem, every decision SAP makes becomes your decision. Keep at least one agent, one MCP server, one model outside the Gateway. Not out of rebellion. Out of optionality.

The Question for This Week

SAP announced the autonomous future. The market said no — at least in the short term. But the direction is clear. In 24 months, what was announced in Orlando will be partially deployed. And the operational question is the same as always.

Are you going to be the executor that AI replaces, or the Operator who decides what gets executed?

That decision is made every day, in every interaction with every AI tool. It's not made in the dramatic moment of the layoff. It's made on a boring Wednesday afternoon when you ask Joule "explain this class to me," and you choose to verify what it answered.

Christian Klein closed his keynote with a line:

The future isn't written by AI — it's written by us.

On that, the CEO of SAP and I agree. Where we differ is on who "us" is. For SAP, it's the company that buys the Suite. For me, it's the professional who operates the system. And the difference between those two "us" is what will define careers over the next five years.

The Operator is not a title you reach. It's a practice you sustain.

P.S. I'm preparing something concrete for Operators. Not for passive AI users. For those with accumulated judgment who want to sharpen it. If you want to be the first to know, subscribe at pablomarichal.com. No spam, no filler. Just the notice when it's ready.

Sources:

SAP Sapphire 2026 keynote, Christian Klein, Orlando, May 2026.

The Roadmap to the Autonomous Enterprise: SAP's 5-Year Strategic Vision (Post-Sapphire 2026), Sapphire Innovation Guide analysis.

SAP SE stock data, ETR: SAP, close May 15, 2026.

Melissa Rosenthal, "Every AI company serving your org is currently losing money on you," LinkedIn, May 2026.

Jon Reed, Diginomica, statements published on LinkedIn, May 2026.

Laban, Schnabel, Neville. "LLMs Corrupt Your Documents When You Delegate." Microsoft Research, April 2026. arXiv:2604.15597.

Previous newsletters referenced: The Operator (May 12), DELEGATE-52 (May 5), The Two SAP Castes (February 2026).
